A '''financial market''' is a market in which people trade financial securities and derivatives at low transaction costs.
[S01627] Some of the securities include stocks and bonds, raw materials and precious metals, which are known in the financial markets as commodities.
[S01628] The term "market" is sometimes used for what are more strictly ''exchanges'', that is, organizations that facilitate the trade in financial securities, e.g., a stock exchange or commodity exchange.
[S01629] This may be a physical location (such as the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE), London Stock Exchange (LSE), Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE), or Johannesburg Stock Exchange (JSE Limited)), or an electronic system such as NASDAQ.
[S01630] Much trading of stocks takes place on an exchange; still, corporate actions (mergers, spinoffs) are outside an exchange, while any two companies or people, for whatever reason, may agree to sell the stock from the one to the other without using an exchange.
[S01631] Trading of currencies and bonds is largely on a bilateral basis, although some bonds trade on a stock exchange, and people are building electronic systems for these as well.
[S01632] Within the financial sector, the term "financial markets" is often used to refer just to the markets that are used to raise finances.
[S01633] For long term finance, they are usually called the capital markets; for short term finance, they are usually called money markets.
[S01634] The money market deals in short-term loans, generally for a period of a year or less.
[S01635] Another common use of the term is as a catchall for all the markets in the financial sector, as per examples in the breakdown below.
[S01636] * Capital markets, which consist of:
**Stock markets, which provide financing through the issuance of shares or common stock, and enable the subsequent trading thereof.
[S01637] **Bond markets, which provide financing through the issuance of bonds, and enable the subsequent trading thereof.
[S01638] * Commodity markets, which facilitate trading in the primary economic sector rather than manufactured products.
[S01639] Soft commodities is a term generally referring to commodities that are grown rather than mined, such as crops (corn, wheat, soybean, fruit and vegetable), livestock, cocoa, coffee and sugar.
[S01640] Hard commodities is a term generally referring to commodities that are mined such as gold, gemstones and other metals and generally drilled such as oil and gas.
[S01641] * Money markets, which provide short term debt financing and investment.
[S01642] * Derivatives markets, which provide instruments for the management of financial risk.
[S01643] * Futures markets, which provide standardized forward contracts for trading products at some future date; see also forward market.
[S01644] * Foreign exchange markets, which facilitate the trading of foreign exchange.
[S01645] * Cryptocurrency markets, which facilitate the trading of digital assets and financial technologies.
[S01646] * Spot market
* Interbank lending market
The capital markets may also be divided into primary markets and secondary markets.
[S01647] Newly formed (issued) securities are bought or sold in primary markets, such as during initial public offerings.
[S01648] Secondary markets allow investors to buy and sell existing securities.
[S01649] The transactions in primary markets exist between issuers and investors, while secondary market transactions exist among investors.
[S01650] Liquidity is a crucial aspect of securities that are traded in secondary markets.
[S01651] Liquidity refers to the ease with which a security can be sold without a loss of value.
[S01652] Securities with an active secondary market mean that there are many buyers and sellers at a given point in time.
[S01653] Investors benefit from liquid securities because they can sell their assets whenever they want; an illiquid security may force the seller to get rid of their asset at a large discount.
[S01654] Financial markets attract funds from investors and channels them to corporations—they thus allow corporations to finance their operations and achieve growth.
[S01655] Money markets allow firms to borrow funds on a short-term basis, while capital markets allow corporations to gain long-term funding to support expansion (known as maturity transformation).
[S01656] "Financial Markets - Overview, Types, and Functions" 

Without financial markets, borrowers would have difficulty finding lenders themselves.
[S01657] Intermediaries such as banks, Investment Banks, and Boutique Investment Banks can help in this process.
[S01658] Banks take deposits from those who have money to save on the form of savings a/c.
[S01659] They can then lend money from this pool of deposited money to those who seek to borrow.
[S01660] Banks popularly lend money in the form of loans and mortgages.
[S01661] More complex transactions than a simple bank deposit require markets where lenders and their agents can meet borrowers and their agents, and where existing borrowing or lending commitments can be sold on to other parties.
[S01662] A good example of a financial market is a stock exchange.
[S01663] A company can raise money by selling shares to investors and its existing shares can be bought or sold.
[S01664] The following table illustrates where financial markets fit in the relationship between lenders and borrowers:

The lender temporarily gives money to somebody else, on the condition of getting back the principal amount together with some interest or profit or charge.
[S01665] Many individuals are not aware that they are lenders, but almost everybody does lend money in many ways.
[S01666] A person lends money when he or she:

* Puts money in a savings account at a bank
* Contributes to a pension plan
* Pays premiums to an insurance company
* Invests in government bonds

''Companies'' tend to be lenders of capital.
[S01667] When companies have surplus cash that is not needed for a short period of time, they may seek to make money from their cash surplus by lending it via short term markets called money markets.
[S01668] Alternatively, such companies may decide to return the cash surplus to their shareholders (e.g.
[S01669] via a share repurchase or dividend payment).
[S01670] Banks can be lenders themselves as they are able to create new debt money in the form of deposits.
[S01671] * ''Individuals'' borrow money via bankers' loans for short term needs or longer term mortgages to help finance a house purchase.
[S01672] * ''Companies'' borrow money to aid short term or long term cash flows.
[S01673] They also borrow to fund modernization or future business expansion.
[S01674] It is common for companies to use mixed packages of different types of funding for different purposes – especially where large complex projects such as company management buyouts are concerned.
[S01675] ''The Business Finance Market: A Survey'' ISR/Google Books, 2013.
[S01676] * ''Governments'' often find their spending requirements exceed their tax revenues.
[S01677] To make up this difference, they need to borrow.
[S01678] Governments also borrow on behalf of nationalized industries, municipalities, local authorities and other public sector bodies.
[S01679] In the UK, the total borrowing requirement is often referred to as the Public sector net cash requirement (PSNCR).
[S01680] Governments borrow by issuing bonds.
[S01681] In the UK, the government also borrows from individuals by offering bank accounts and Premium Bonds.
[S01682] Government debt seems to be permanent.
[S01683] Indeed, the debt seemingly expands rather than being paid off.
[S01684] One strategy used by governments to reduce the ''value'' of the debt is to influence ''inflation''.
[S01685] ''Municipalities and local authorities'' may borrow in their own name as well as receiving funding from national governments.
[S01686] In the UK, this would cover an authority like Hampshire County Council.
[S01687] ''Public Corporations'' typically include nationalized industries.
[S01688] These may include the postal services, railway companies and utility companies.
[S01689] Many borrowers have difficulty raising money locally.
[S01690] They need to borrow internationally with the aid of Foreign exchange markets.
[S01691] Borrowers having similar needs can form into a group of borrowers.
[S01692] They can also take an organizational form like Mutual Funds.
[S01693] They can provide mortgage on weight basis.
[S01694] The main advantage is that this lowers the cost of their borrowings.
[S01695] During the 1980s and 1990s, a major growth sector in financial markets was the trade in so called '''derivatives'''.
[S01696] In the financial markets, stock prices, share prices, bond prices, currency rates, interest rates and dividends go up and down, creating ''risk''.
[S01697] Derivative products are financial products that are used to ''control'' risk or paradoxically ''exploit'' risk.
[S01698] Robert E. Wright and Vincenzo Quadrini.
[S01699] Money and Banking: "Chapter 2, Section 4: Financial Markets."
[S01700] pp.
[S01701] 3 Accessed June 20, 2012 It is also called financial economics.
[S01702] Derivative products or instruments help the issuers to gain an unusual profit from issuing the instruments.
[S01703] For using the help of these products a contract has to be made.
[S01704] Derivative contracts are mainly four types: 
# Future
# Forward
# Option
# Swap

Over the past few decades, the derivatives market has increased and become essential to the financial industry.
[S01705] As the market expands, establishing and improving the regulatory framework becomes particularly critical.
[S01706] In response to the systemic risks exposed by the global economic crisis in 2008, essential regulations such as the Dodd-Frank Act (US) and the EU Market Fundamentals Regulation (MiFID II) were enacted.
[S01707] * The Dodd-Frank Act focuses on increasing transparency and regulating the derivatives market, particularly over-the-counter derivatives transactions, requiring clearing through central counterparties.
[S01708] * MiFID II enhances the market's efficiency, transparency, and fairness, improving transaction transparency and strengthening investor protection.
[S01709] These regulations have significantly changed the market structure and strengthened supervision and risk management of the derivatives market.
[S01710] Although regulatory measures have enhanced market stability, they have also had a broad impact on market participants' operating models and strategies.
[S01711] Seemingly, the most obvious buyers and sellers of currency are importers and exporters of goods.
[S01712] While this may have been true in the distant past, when international trade created the demand for currency markets, importers and exporters now represent only 1/32 of foreign exchange dealing, according to the Bank for International Settlements.
[S01713] Steven Valdez, ''An Introduction To Global Financial Markets'' 

The picture of foreign currency transactions today shows:
* Banks/Institutions
* Speculators
* Government spending (for example, military bases abroad)
* Importers/Exporters
* Tourists

Much effort has gone into the study of financial markets and how prices vary with time.
[S01714] Charles Dow, one of the founders of Dow Jones & Company and The Wall Street Journal, enunciated a set of ideas on the subject which are now called Dow theory.
[S01715] This is the basis of the so-called technical analysis method of attempting to predict future changes.
[S01716] One of the tenets of "technical analysis" is that market trends give an indication of the future, at least in the short term.
[S01717] The claims of the technical analysts are disputed by many academics, who claim that the evidence points rather to the random walk hypothesis, which states that the next change is not correlated to the last change.
[S01718] The role of human psychology in price variations also plays a significant factor.
[S01719] Large amounts of volatility often indicate the presence of strong emotional factors playing into the price.
[S01720] Fear can cause excessive drops in price and greed can create bubbles.
[S01721] In recent years the rise of algorithmic and high-frequency program trading has seen the adoption of momentum, ultra-short term moving average and other similar strategies which are based on technical as opposed to fundamental or theoretical concepts of market behaviour.
[S01722] For instance, according to a study published by the European Central Bank, high frequency trading has a substantial correlation with news announcements and other relevant public information that are able to create wide price movements (e.g., interest rates decisions, trade of balances etc.)
[S01723] The scale of changes in price over some unit of time is called the volatility.
[S01724] It was discovered by Benoit Mandelbrot that changes in prices do not follow a normal distribution, but are rather modeled better by Lévy stable distributions.
[S01725] The scale of change, or volatility, depends on the length of the time unit to a power a bit more than 1/2.
[S01726] Large changes up or down are more likely than what one would calculate using a normal distribution with an estimated standard deviation.
[S01727] *'''Poison pill''', when a company issues more shares to prevent being bought out by another company, thereby increasing the number of outstanding shares to be bought by the hostile company making the bid to establish majority.
[S01728] *'''Bips''', meaning "bps" or basis points.
[S01729] A basis point is a financial unit of measurement used to describe the magnitude of percent change in a variable.
[S01730] One basis point is the equivalent of one hundredth of a percent.
[S01731] For example, if a stock price were to rise 100bit/s, it means it would increase 1%.
[S01732] * '''Quant''', a quantitative analyst with advanced training in mathematics and statistical methods.
[S01733] * '''Rocket scientist''', a financial consultant at the zenith of mathematical and computer programming skill.
[S01734] They are able to invent derivatives of high complexity and construct sophisticated pricing models.
[S01735] They generally handle the most advanced computing techniques adopted by the financial markets since the early 1980s.
[S01736] Typically, they are physicists and engineers by training.
[S01737] *'''IPO''', stands for initial public offering, which is the process a new private company goes through to "go public" or become a publicly traded company on some index.
[S01738] * '''White Knight''', a friendly party in a takeover bid.
[S01739] Used to describe a party that buys the shares of one organization to help prevent against a hostile takeover of that organization by another party.
[S01740] * Round-tripping
* '''Smurfing''', a deliberate structuring of payments or transactions to conceal it from regulators or other parties, a type of money laundering that is often illegal.
[S01741] * '''Bid–ask spread''', the difference between the highest bid and the lowest offer.
[S01742] * '''Pip''', smallest price move that a given exchange rate makes based on market convention.
[S01743] *'''Pegging''', when a country wants to obtain price stability, it can use pegging to fix their exchange rate relative to another currency.
[S01744] *'''Bearish''', this phrase is used to refer to the fact that the market has a downward trend.
[S01745] *'''Bullish''', this term is used to refer to the fact that the market has an upward trend.
[S01746] * '''Intermediary functions''': The intermediary functions of financial markets include the following:
** '''Transfer of resources''': Financial markets facilitate the transfer of real economic resources from lenders to ultimate borrowers.
[S01747] ** '''Enhancing income''': Financial markets allow lenders to earn interest or dividend on their surplus invisible funds, thus contributing to the enhancement of the individual and the national income.
[S01748] ** '''Productive usage''': Financial markets allow for the productive use of the funds borrowed.
[S01749] The enhancing the income and the gross national production.
[S01750] ** '''Capital formation''': Financial markets provide a channel through which new savings flow to aid capital formation of a country.
[S01751] ** '''Price determination''': Financial markets allow for the determination of price of the traded financial assets through the interaction of buyers and sellers.
[S01752] They provide a sign for the allocation of funds in the economy based on the demand and to the supply through the mechanism called price discovery process.
[S01753] ** '''Sale mechanism''': Financial markets provide a mechanism for selling of a financial asset by an investor so as to offer the benefit of marketability and liquidity of such assets.
[S01754] ** '''Information''': The activities of the participants in the financial market result in the generation and the consequent dissemination of information to the various segments of the market.
[S01755] So as to reduce the cost of transaction of financial assets.
[S01756] * '''Financial Functions'''
** Providing the borrower with funds so as to enable them to carry out their investment plans.
[S01757] ** Providing the lenders with earning assets so as to enable them to earn wealth by deploying the assets in production debentures.
[S01758] ** Providing liquidity in the market so as to facilitate trading of funds.
[S01759] ** Providing liquidity to commercial bank
** Facilitating credit creation
** Promoting savings
** Promoting investment
** Facilitating balanced economic growth
** Improving trading floors

* '''Primary market''': A primary market is a market for new issues or new financial claims.
[S01760] Therefore, it is also called new issue market.
[S01761] The primary market deals with those securities which are issued to the public for the first time.
[S01762] * '''Secondary market''': A market for secondary sale of securities.
[S01763] In other words, securities which have already passed through the new issue market are traded in this market.
[S01764] Generally, such securities are quoted in the stock exchange and it provides a continuous and regular market for buying and selling of securities.
[S01765] Simply put, primary market is the market where the newly started company issued shares to the public for the first time through IPO (initial public offering).
[S01766] Secondary market is the market where the second hand securities are sold (security Commodity Markets).
[S01767] * '''Money market''': Money market is a market for dealing with the financial assets and securities which have a maturity period of up to one year.
[S01768] In other words, it is a market for purely short-term funds.
[S01769] * '''Capital market''': A capital market is a market for financial assets that have a long or indefinite maturity.
[S01770] Generally, it deals with long-term securities that have a maturity period of above one year.
[S01771] The capital market may be further divided into (a) industrial securities market (b) Govt.
[S01772] securities market and (c) long-term loans market.
[S01773] ** '''Equity markets''': A market where ownership of securities are issued and subscribed is known as equity market.
[S01774] An example of a secondary equity market for shares is the New York (NYSE) stock exchange.
[S01775] ** '''Debt market''': The market where funds are borrowed and lent is known as debt market.
[S01776] Arrangements are made in such a way that the borrowers agree to pay the lender the original amount of the loan plus some specified amount of interest.
[S01777] * '''Derivative markets''': A market where financial instruments are derived and traded based on an underlying asset such as commodities or stocks.
[S01778] * '''Financial service market''': A market that comprises participants such as commercial banks that provide various financial services like ATM.
[S01779] Credit cards.
[S01780] Credit rating, stock broking etc.
[S01781] is known as financial service market.
[S01782] Individuals and firms use financial services markets, to purchase services that enhance the workings of debt and equity markets.
[S01783] * '''Depository markets''': A depository market consists of depository institutions (such as banks) that accept deposits from individuals and firms and uses these funds to participate in the debt market, by giving loans or purchasing other debt instruments such as treasury bills.
[S01784] * '''Non-depository market''': Non-depository market carry out various functions in financial markets ranging from financial intermediary to selling, insurance etc.
[S01785] The various constituencies in non-depositary markets are mutual funds, insurance companies, pension funds, brokerage firms etc.
[S01786] *'''Relation between Bonds and Commodity Prices''': With the increase in commodity prices, the cost of goods for companies increases.
[S01787] This increase in commodity prices level causes a rise in inflation.
[S01788] *'''Relation between Commodities and Equities''': Due to the production cost remaining same, and revenues rising (due to high commodity prices), the operating profit (revenue minus cost) increases, which in turn drives up equity prices.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
in Lower Manhattan, New York City is the world's largest stock exchange per total market capitalization of its listed companies. ]]
[S01833] A '''stock exchange''', '''securities exchange''', or '''bourse''' is an exchange where stockbrokers and traders can buy and sell securities, such as shares of stock, bonds and other financial instruments.
[S01834] Stock exchanges may also provide facilities for the issue and redemption of such securities and instruments and capital events including the payment of income and dividends.
[S01835] Securities traded on a stock exchange include stock issued by listed companies, unit trusts, derivatives, pooled investment products and bonds.
[S01836] Stock exchanges often function as "continuous auction" markets with buyers and sellers consummating transactions via open outcry at a central location such as the floor of the exchange or by using an electronic system to process financial transactions.
[S01837] Lemke and Lins, ''Soft Dollars and Other Trading Activities'', §2:3 (Thomson West, 2013–2014 ed.).
[S01838] To be able to trade a security on a particular stock exchange, the security must be listed there.
[S01839] Usually, there is a central location for record keeping, but trade is increasingly less linked to a physical place as modern markets use electronic communication networks, which give them advantages of increased speed and reduced cost of transactions.
[S01840] Trade on an exchange is restricted to brokers who are members of the exchange.
[S01841] In recent years, various other trading venues such as electronic communication networks, alternative trading systems and "dark pools" have taken much of the trading activity away from traditional stock exchanges.
[S01842] Lemke and Lins, ''Soft Dollars and Other Trading Activities'', §§2:25 – 2:30 (Thomson West, 2013–2014 ed.).
[S01843] Initial public offerings of stocks and bonds to investors is done in the primary market and subsequent trading is done in the secondary market.
[S01844] A stock exchange is often the most important component of a stock market.
[S01845] Supply and demand in stock markets are driven by various factors that, as in all free markets, affect the price of stocks (see stock valuation).
[S01846] There is usually no obligation for stock to be issued through the stock exchange itself, nor must stock be subsequently traded on an exchange.
[S01847] Such trading may be ''off-exchange'' or over-the-counter.
[S01848] This is the usual way that derivatives and bonds are traded.
[S01849] Increasingly, stock exchanges are part of a global securities market.
[S01850] Stock exchanges also serve an economic function in providing liquidity to shareholders in providing an efficient means of disposing of shares.
[S01851] In recent years, as the ease and speed of exchanging stocks over digital platforms has increased, volatility in the day-to-day market has increased, too.
[S01852] The beginnings of lending were in Italy in the late Middle Ages.
[S01853] In the 14th century, Venetian lenders would carry slates with information on the various issues for sale and meet with clients, much like a broker does today.
[S01854] Venetian merchants introduced the principle of exchanging debts between moneylenders; a lender looking to unload a high-risk, high-interest loan might exchange it for a different loan with another lender.
[S01855] These lenders also bought government debt issues.
[S01856] As the natural evolution of their business continued, the lenders began to sell debt issues to the first individual investors.
[S01857] The Venetians were the leaders in the field and the first to start trading securities from other governments, yet did not embark on private trade with India.
[S01858] Nor did the Italians connect on land with the Chinese Silk Road.
[S01859] Along the potential overland trade route, Holy Roman Emperor Frederick II repulsed advances by Mongol Batu Khan (Golden Horde) in 1241.
[S01860] There is little consensus among scholars as to when corporate stock was first traded.
[S01861] Some view the key event as the Dutch East India Company's founding in 1602, while others point to much earlier developments (Bruges, Antwerp in 1531 and in Lyon in 1548).
[S01862] The first book in history of securities exchange, the Confusion of Confusions, was written by the Dutch-Jewish trader Joseph de la Vega and the Amsterdam Stock Exchange is often considered the oldest "modern" securities market in the world.
[S01863] On the other hand, economist Ulrike Malmendier of the University of California at Berkeley argues that a share market existed as far back as ancient Rome, that derives from Etruscan "Argentari".
[S01864] In the Roman Republic, which existed for centuries before the Empire was founded, there were ''societates publicanorum'', organizations of contractors or leaseholders who performed temple-building and other services for the government.
[S01865] One such service was the feeding of geese on the Capitoline Hill as a reward to the birds after their honking warned of a Gallic invasion in 390&nbsp;B.C.
[S01866] Participants in such organizations had ''partes'' or shares, a concept mentioned various times by the statesman and orator Cicero.
[S01867] In one speech, Cicero mentions "shares that had a very high price at the time".
[S01868] Such evidence, in Malmendier's view, suggests the instruments were tradable, with fluctuating values based on an organization's success.
[S01869] The ''societas'' declined into obscurity in the time of the emperors, as most of their services were taken over by direct agents of the state.
[S01870] Tradable bonds as a commonly used type of security were a more recent innovation, spearheaded by the Italian city-states of the late medieval and early Renaissance periods.
[S01871] Stringham, Edward Peter; Curott, Nicholas A.: ''On the Origins of Stock Markets'' [Part IV: ''Institutions and Organizations''; Chapter 14], pp.
[S01872] 324–344, in ''The Oxford Handbook of Austrian Economics'', edited by Peter J. Boettke and Christopher J. Coyne.
[S01873] (Oxford University Press, 2015, ).
[S01874] Edward P. Stringham & Nicholas A. Curott: "Business ventures with multiple shareholders became popular with ''commenda'' contracts in medieval Italy (Greif, 2006, p. 286), and Malmendier (2009) provides evidence that shareholder companies date back to ancient Rome.
[S01875] Yet the title of the world's first stock market deservedly goes to that of seventeenth-century Amsterdam, where an active secondary market in company shares emerged.
[S01876] The two major companies were the Dutch East India Company and the Dutch West India Company, founded in 1602 and 1621.
[S01877] Other companies existed, but they were not as large and constituted a small portion of the stock market (Israel [1989] 1991, 109–112; Dehing and 't Hart 1997, 54; dela Vega [1688] 1996, 173)."
[S01878] ]]
Joseph de la Vega, also known as Joseph Penso de la Vega and by other variations of his name, was an Amsterdam trader from a Spanish Jewish family and a prolific writer as well as a successful businessman in 17th-century Amsterdam.
[S01879] His 1688 book ''Confusion of Confusions'' De la Vega, Joseph, ''Confusion de Confusiones'' (1688), ''Portions Descriptive of the Amsterdam Stock Exchange'', introduction by Hermann Kellenbenz, Baker Library, Harvard Graduate School of Business Administration (1957) explained the workings of the city's stock market.
[S01880] It was the earliest book about stock trading and inner workings of a stock market, taking the form of a dialogue between a merchant, a shareholder and a philosopher, the book described a market that was sophisticated but also prone to excesses, and de la Vega offered advice to his readers on such topics as the unpredictability of market shifts and the importance of patience in investment.
[S01881] in 1810]]
In England, London's first stockbrokers were barred from the old commercial center known as the Royal Exchange, reportedly because of their rude manners, in the late 17th century.
[S01882] Instead, the new trade was conducted from coffee houses along Exchange Alley.
[S01883] By 1698, a broker named John Castaing, operating out of Jonathan's Coffee House, was posting regular lists of stock and commodity prices.
[S01884] Those lists mark the beginning of the London Stock Exchange.
[S01885] One of history's greatest financial bubbles occurred around 1720.
[S01886] At the center of it were the South Sea Company, set up in 1711 to conduct English trade with South America, and the Mississippi Company, focused on commerce with France's Louisiana colony and touted by transplanted Scottish financier John Law, who was acting in effect as France's central banker.
[S01887] Investors snapped up shares in both, and whatever else was available.
[S01888] In 1720, at the height of the mania, there was even an offering of "a company for carrying out an undertaking of great advantage, but nobody to know what it is".
[S01889] By the end of that same year, share prices had started collapsing, as it became clear that expectations of imminent wealth from the Americas were overblown.
[S01890] In London, Parliament passed the Bubble Act, which stated that only royally chartered companies could issue public shares.
[S01891] In Paris, Law was stripped of office and fled the country.
[S01892] Stock trading was more limited and subdued in subsequent decades.
[S01893] Yet the market survived, and by the 1790s shares were being traded in the young United States.
[S01894] On May 17, 1792, the New York Stock Exchange opened under a ''Platanus occidentalis'' (buttonwood tree) in New York City, as 24 stockbrokers signed the Buttonwood Agreement, agreeing to trade five securities under that buttonwood tree. 

)
[S01895] in 1875 acting as Bombay Stock Exchange]]
Bombay Stock Exchange was started by Premchand Roychand in 1875.
[S01896] While BSE Limited is now synonymous with Dalal Street, it was not always so.
[S01897] In the 1850s, five stock brokers gathered together under a Banyan tree in front of Mumbai Town Hall, where Horniman Circle is now situated.
[S01898] A decade later, the brokers moved their location to another leafy setting, this time under banyan trees at the junction of Meadows Street and what was then called Esplanade Road, now Mahatma Gandhi Road.
[S01899] With a rapid increase in the number of brokers, they had to shift places repeatedly.
[S01900] At last, in 1874, the brokers found a permanent location, the one that they could call their own.
[S01901] The brokers group became an official organization known as "The Native Share & Stock Brokers Association" in 1875.
[S01902] The Bombay Stock Exchange continued to operate out of a building near the Town Hall until 1928.
[S01903] The present site near Horniman Circle was acquired by the exchange in 1928, and a building was constructed and occupied in 1930.
[S01904] The street on which the site is located came to be called ''Dalal Street'' in Hindi (meaning "Broker Street") due to the location of the exchange.
[S01905] On 31 August 1957, the BSE became the first stock exchange to be recognized by the Indian Government under the Securities Contracts Regulation Act.
[S01906] Construction of the present building, the Phiroze Jeejeebhoy Towers at Dalal Street, Fort area, began in the late 1970s and was completed and occupied by the BSE in 1980.
[S01907] Initially named the ''BSE Towers'', the name of the building was changed soon after occupation, in memory of Sir Phiroze Jamshedji Jeejeebhoy, chairman of the BSE since 1966, following his death.
[S01908] In 1986, the BSE developed the S&P BSE SENSEX index, giving the BSE a means to measure the overall performance of the exchange.
[S01909] In 2000, the BSE used this index to open its derivatives market, trading S&P BSE SENSEX futures contracts.
[S01910] The development of S&P BSE SENSEX options along with equity derivatives followed in 2001 and 2002, expanding the BSE's trading platform.
[S01911] Historically an open outcry floor trading exchange, the Bombay Stock Exchange switched to an electronic trading system developed by Cmc ltd. in 1995.
[S01912] It took the exchange only 50 days to make this transition.
[S01913] This automated, screen-based trading platform called BSE On-Line Trading (BOLT) had a capacity of 8 million orders per day.
[S01914] Now BSE has raised capital by issuing shares and as on 3 May 2017 the BSE share which is traded in NSE only closed with ₹999.
[S01915] in New York City, US, is the largest stock exchange in the world.]]
[S01916] in New York City, US, is the second-largest stock exchange in the world.]]
[S01917] in Shanghai, China, is the third-largest stock exchange in the world.]]
[S01918] in Tokyo, Japan, is the fourth-largest stock exchange in the world.]]
[S01919] in Hong Kong, is the fifth-largest stock exchange in the world.]]
[S01920] in Amsterdam, Netherlands, for the European Union is the sixth-largest stock exchange in the world.]]
[S01921] in Mumbai, India, is the seventh-largest stock exchange in the world, oldest and fourth-largest in Asia, largest in India.
[S01922] It is the fastest stock exchange in the world.]]
[S01923] in Mumbai, India, is the eight-largest stock exchange in the world, fifth-largest in Asia and second-largest in India.]]
[S01924] in Shenzhen, China, is the ninth-largest stock exchange in the world, sixth-largest in Asia and second-largest in China.]]
[S01925] in Toronto, Canada, is the tenth-largest stock exchange in the world and third-largest in North America.]]
[S01926] in London, UK, is the eleventh-largest stock exchange in the world, largest non-EU European Stock Exchange and second largest in Europe.]]
[S01927] in Sydney, is the largest stock exchange in Oceania.]]
[S01928] in São Paulo, Brazil, is the largest stock exchange in South America.]]
[S01929] in Johannesburg, South Africa, is the largest stock exchange in Africa.]]
[S01930] Stock exchanges have multiple roles in the economy.
[S01931] This may include the following: 

Besides the borrowing capacity provided to an individual or firm by the banking system, in the form of credit or a loan, a stock exchange provides companies with the facility to raise capital for expansion through selling shares to the investing public.
[S01932] Capital intensive companies, particularly high tech companies, typically need to raise high volumes of capital in their early stages.
[S01933] For this reason, the public market provided by the stock exchanges has been one of the most important funding sources for many capital intensive startups.
[S01934] In the 1990s and early 2000s, hi-tech listed companies experienced a boom and bust in the world's major stock exchanges.
[S01935] Since then, it has been much more demanding for the high-tech entrepreneur to take his/her company public, unless either the company is already generating sales and earnings, or the company has demonstrated credibility and potential from successful outcomes: clinical trials, market research, patent registrations, etc.
[S01936] This shift in market expectations has led to an increased reliance on private equity and venture capital funding in the early stages of high-tech companies.
[S01937] This is quite different from the situation of the 1990s to early-2000s period, when a number of companies (particularly Internet boom and biotechnology companies) went public in the most prominent stock exchanges around the world in the total absence of sales, earnings, or any type of well-documented promising outcome.
[S01938] Though it is not as common, it still happens that highly speculative and financially unpredictable hi-tech startups are listed for the first time in a major stock exchange.
[S01939] Additionally, there are smaller, specialized entry markets for these kind of companies with stock indexes tracking their performance (examples include the Alternext, CAC Small, SDAX, TecDAX).
[S01940] Alternative investment funds refer to funds that include '''hedge funds, venture capital, private equity, angel funds, real estate, commodities, collectibles, structured products''', etc.
[S01941] Alternative investment funds are an alternative to traditional investment options (stocks, bonds, and cash).
[S01942] Companies have also raised significant amounts of capital through R&D limited partnerships.
[S01943] Tax law changes that were enacted in 1987 in the United States changed the tax deductibility of investments in R&D limited partnerships.
[S01944] In order for a partnership to be of interest to investors today, the cash on cash return must be high enough to entice investors.
[S01945] A general source of capital for startup companies has been venture capital.
[S01946] This source remains largely available today, but the maximum statistical amount that the venture company firms in aggregate will invest in any one company is not limitless (it was approximately $15&nbsp;million in 2001 for a biotechnology company).
[S01947] Another alternative source of cash for a private company is a corporate partner, usually an established multinational company, which provides capital for the smaller company in return for marketing rights, patent rights, or equity.
[S01948] Corporate partnerships have been used successfully in a large number of cases.
[S01949] When people draw their savings and invest in shares (through an initial public offering or the seasoned equity offering of an already listed company), it usually leads to rational allocation of resources because funds, which could have been consumed, or kept in idle deposits with banks, are mobilized and redirected to help companies' management boards finance their organizations.
[S01950] This may promote business activity with benefits for several economic sectors such as agriculture, commerce and industry, resulting in stronger economic growth and higher productivity levels of firms.
[S01951] Companies view acquisitions as an opportunity to expand product lines, increase distribution channels, hedge against volatility, increase their market share, or acquire other necessary business assets.
[S01952] A takeover bid or mergers and acquisitions through the stock market is one of the simplest and most common ways for a company to grow by acquisition or fusion.
[S01953] By going public and listing on a stock exchange, companies gain access to a broader pool of investors, which can provide the necessary funds for expansion, research and development, and other growth initiatives.
[S01954] Additionally, being listed on a stock exchange enhances a company's visibility and credibility, making it more attractive to potential partners, customers, and employees.
[S01955] According to a report by the World Federation of Exchanges (WFE), stock exchanges contribute to economic growth by enabling companies to access long-term capital, thereby fostering innovation and job creation.
[S01956] While stock exchanges are not designed to be platforms for the redistribution of wealth, they play a significant role in allowing both casual and professional stock investors to partake in the wealth generated by profitable businesses.
[S01957] This is achieved through the distribution of dividends and the potential for stock price increases leading to capital gains.
[S01958] As a result, individuals who invest in stocks have the opportunity to share in the prosperity of successful companies, effectively participating in a form of wealth redistribution through their investment activities.
[S01959] Thus, while not the primary purpose of stock exchanges, the opportunity for individuals to benefit from the success of businesses can be seen as a form of wealth redistribution within the financial markets.
[S01960] Both casual and professional stock investors, as large as institutional investors or as small as an ordinary middle-class family, through dividends and stock price increases that may result in capital gains, share in the wealth of profitable businesses.
[S01961] Unprofitable and troubled businesses may result in capital losses for shareholders.
[S01962] By having a wide and varied scope of owners, companies generally tend to improve management standards and efficiency to satisfy the demands of these shareholders and the more stringent rules for public corporations imposed by public stock exchanges and the government.
[S01963] This improvement can be attributed in some cases to the price mechanism exerted through shares of stock, wherein the price of the stock falls when management is considered poor (making the firm vulnerable to a takeover by new management) or rises when management is doing well (making the firm less vulnerable to a takeover).
[S01964] In addition, publicly listed shares are subject to greater transparency so that investors can make informed decisions about a purchase.
[S01965] Consequently, it is alleged that public companies (companies that are owned by shareholders who are members of the general public and trade shares on public exchanges) tend to have better management records than privately held companies (those companies where shares are not publicly traded, often owned by the company founders, their families and heirs, or otherwise by a small group of investors).
[S01966] Despite this claim, some well-documented cases are known where it is alleged that there has been considerable slippage in corporate governance on the part of some public companies, particularly in the cases of accounting scandals.
[S01967] The policies that led to the dot-com bubble in the late 1990s and the subprime mortgage crisis in 2007–08 are also examples of alleged corporate mismanagement.
[S01968] The alleged mismanagement of companies such as Pets.com (2000), Enron (2001), One.Tel (2001), Sunbeam Products (2001), Webvan (2001), Adelphia Communications Corporation (2002), MCI WorldCom (2002), Parmalat (2003), American International Group (2008), Bear Stearns (2008), Lehman Brothers (2008), General Motors (2009) and Satyam Computer Services (2009) all received plenty of media attention.
[S01969] Many banks and companies worldwide use securities identification numbers (ISIN) to identify, uniquely, their stocks, bonds and other securities.
[S01970] Adding an ISIN code helps to distinctly identify securities and the ISIN system is used worldwide by funds, companies, and governments.
[S01971] However, when poor financial, ethical or managerial records become public, stock investors tend to lose money as the stock and the company tend to lose value.
[S01972] In the stock exchanges, shareholders of underperforming firms are often penalized by significant share price decline, and they tend as well to dismiss incompetent management teams.
[S01973] As opposed to other businesses that require huge capital outlay, investing in shares is open to both the large and small stock investors as minimum investment amounts are minimal.
[S01974] Therefore, the stock exchange provides the opportunity for small investors to own shares of the same companies as large investors.
[S01975] As opposed to other businesses that require huge capital outlay, investing in shares is open to both the large and small stock investors as minimum investment amounts are minimal.
[S01976] Therefore, the stock exchange provides the opportunity for small investors to own shares of the same companies as large investors.
[S01977] Governments at various levels may decide to borrow money to finance infrastructure projects such as sewage and water treatment works or housing estates by selling another category of securities known as bonds.
[S01978] These bonds can be raised through the stock exchange whereby members of the public buy them, thus loaning money to the government.
[S01979] The issuance of such bonds can obviate, in the short term, direct taxation of citizens to finance development—though by securing such bonds with the full faith and credit of the government instead of with collateral, the government must eventually tax citizens or otherwise raise additional funds to make any regular coupon payments and refund the principal when the bonds mature.
[S01980] At the stock exchange, share prices rise and decreases depending, largely, on economic forces.
[S01981] Share prices tend to rise or remain stable when companies and the economy in general show signs of stability and growth.
[S01982] A recession, depression, or financial crisis could eventually lead to a stock market crash.
[S01983] Therefore, the movement of share prices and in general of the stock indexes can be an indicator of the general trend in the economy.
[S01984] Stock exchanges offer employment opportunities to various individuals such as jobbers and other members who perform activities within the stock exchange.
[S01985] This makes the stock exchange a source of employment, not only for investors but also for the members and their employees.
[S01986] The diverse range of roles within the stock exchange, including trading, analysis, compliance, and administrative functions, creates an ecosystem of employment opportunities that support the operations and functions of the exchange.
[S01987] Additionally, the stock exchange's role in facilitating capital formation and investment in businesses also indirectly contributes to job creation and economic growth, making it a significant player in the employment landscape.
[S01988] The stock exchange plays a role in regulating companies by exerting a significant influence on their management practices.
[S01989] To be listed on a stock exchange, a company is required to adhere to a set of rules and regulations established by the exchange itself.
[S01990] These regulations serve as a framework for corporate governance, financial transparency, and accountability, thereby ensuring that listed companies operate in a manner that is conducive to investor confidence and market stability.
[S01991] By imposing these standards, stock exchanges contribute to the overall integrity and reliability of the financial markets, fostering an environment where companies are held accountable for their actions and decisions, ultimately benefiting both investors and the broader economy.
[S01992] Each stock exchange imposes its own listing requirements upon companies that want to be listed on that exchange.
[S01993] Such conditions may include minimum number of shares outstanding, minimum market capitalization, and minimum annual income.
[S01994] The listing requirements imposed by some stock exchanges include:
* '''New York Stock Exchange:''' the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) requires a company to have issued at least 1.1&nbsp;million shares of stock worth $40&nbsp;million and must have earned more than $10&nbsp;million over the last three years.
[S01995] * '''NASDAQ Stock Exchange:''' NASDAQ requires a company to have issued at least 1.25&nbsp;million shares of stock worth at least $70&nbsp;million and must have earned more than $11&nbsp;million over the last three years.
[S01996] * '''London Stock Exchange:''' the main market of the London Stock Exchange requires a minimum market capitalization (£700,000), three years of audited financial statements, minimum public float (25%) and sufficient working capital for at least 12 months from the date of listing.
[S01997] * '''Bombay Stock Exchange:''' Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE) requires a minimum market capitalization of and minimum public float equivalent to .
[S01998] * '''The Shanghai Stock Exchange (SSE):''' To be eligible for an initial public offering (IPO) on the Shanghai Stock Exchange SSE, a company must meet certain criteria such as minimum market capitalization, a minimum net profit, and a minimum number of shareholders.
[S01999] Also, the company's total share capital must not be less than RMB 30 million.
[S02000] Companies must also submit financial reports and undergo a review by the CSRC.
[S02001] * '''Australian Securities Exchange in Sydney:''' Australia Securities Exchange in Sydney requires a company to meet the Profit Test by demonstrating either of the following: A$1 million aggregated profit from continuing operations over the past 3 years or A$500,000 consolidated profit from continuing operations over the last 12 months.
[S02002] Alternatively, a company can meet the Assets Test by fulfilling one of the following criteria: A$4 million net tangible assets or A$15 million market capitalization.
[S02003] Stock exchanges originated as mutual organizations, owned by its member stockbrokers.
[S02004] However, the major stock exchanges have ''demutualized'', where the members sell their shares in an initial public offering.
[S02005] In this way the mutual organization becomes a corporation, with shares that are listed on a stock exchange.
[S02006] Examples are Australian Securities Exchange (1998), Euronext (merged with New York Stock Exchange), NASDAQ (2002), Bursa Malaysia (2004), the New York Stock Exchange (2005), , and the São Paulo Stock Exchange (2007).
[S02007] The Shenzhen Stock Exchange and Shanghai Stock Exchange can be characterized as quasi-state institutions insofar as they were created by government bodies in China and their leading personnel are directly appointed by the China Securities Regulatory Commission.
[S02008] Another example is Tashkent Stock Exchange established in 1994, three years after the collapse of the Soviet Union, mainly state-owned but has a form of a public corporation (joint-stock company).
[S02009] Korea Exchange (KRX) owns 25% less one share of the Tashkent Stock Exchange.
[S02010] In 2018, there were 15 licensed stock exchanges in the United States, of which 13 actively traded securities.
[S02011] All of these exchanges were owned by three publicly traded multinational companies, Intercontinental Exchange, Nasdaq, Inc., and Cboe Global Markets, except one, IEX.
[S02012] (for recent history see also, , and In 2019, a group of financial corporations announced plans to open a members owned exchange, MEMX, an ownership structure similar to the mutual organizations of earlier exchanges.
[S02013] '''Top ten traditional stock exchanges by total market capitalization (as of November 2025)'''

In the 19th century, exchanges were opened to trade forward contracts on commodities.
[S02014] Exchange traded forward contracts are called futures contracts.
[S02015] These ''commodity markets'' later started offering future contracts on other products, such as interest rates and shares, as well as options contracts.
[S02016] They are now generally known as futures exchanges.
