──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SOURCE: Wikipedia — Equity (finance)
WORDS: 1,466
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
In finance, '''equity''' is an ownership interest in property that may be subject to debts or other liabilities.
[S04295] Equity is measured for accounting purposes by subtracting liabilities from the value of the assets owned.
[S04296] For example, if someone owns a car worth $24,000 and owes $10,000 on the loan used to buy the car, the difference of $14,000 is equity.
[S04297] Equity can apply to a single asset, such as a car or house, or to an entire business.
[S04298] A business that needs to start up or expand its operations can sell its equity in order to raise cash that does not have to be repaid on a set schedule.
[S04299] When liabilities attached to an asset exceed its value, the difference is called a deficit and the asset is informally said to be "underwater" or "upside-down".
[S04300] In government finance or other non-profit settings, equity is known as "net position" or "net assets".
[S04301] The term "equity" describes this type of ownership in English because it was regulated through the system of equity law that developed in England during the Late Middle Ages to meet the growing demands of commercial activity.
[S04302] While the older common law courts dealt with questions of property title, equity courts dealt with contractual interests in property.
[S04303] The same asset could have an owner in equity, who held the contractual interest, and a separate owner at law, who held the title indefinitely or until the contract was fulfilled.
[S04304] Contract disputes were examined with consideration of whether the terms and administration of the contract were fair—that is, equitable.
[S04305] Any asset that is purchased through a secured loan is said to have equity.
[S04306] While the loan remains unpaid, the buyer does not fully own the asset.
[S04307] The lender has the right to repossess it if the buyer defaults, but only to recover the unpaid loan balance.
[S04308] The equity balance—the asset's market value reduced by the loan balance—measures the buyer's partial ownership.
[S04309] This may be different from the total amount that the buyer has paid on the loan, which includes interest expense and does not consider any change in the asset's value.
[S04310] When an asset has a deficit instead of equity, the terms of the loan determine whether the lender can recover it from the borrower.
[S04311] Houses are normally financed with non-recourse loans, in which the lender assumes a risk that the owner will default with a deficit, while other assets are financed with full-recourse loans that make the borrower responsible for any deficit.
[S04312] The equity of an asset can be used to secure additional liabilities.
[S04313] Common examples include home equity loans and home equity lines of credit.
[S04314] These increase the total liabilities attached to the asset and decrease the owner's equity.
[S04315] A business entity has a more complicated debt structure than a single asset.
[S04316] While some liabilities may be secured by specific assets of the business, others may be guaranteed by the assets of the entire business.
[S04317] If the business becomes bankrupt, it can be required to raise money by selling assets.
[S04318] Yet the equity of the business, like the equity of an asset, approximately measures the amount of the assets that belongs to the owners of the business.
[S04319] In financial accounting, the equity is derived by subtracting its liabilities from its assets.
[S04320] For a business as a whole, this value is sometimes referred to as '''total equity''', to distinguish it from the equity of a single asset.
[S04321] The fundamental accounting equation requires that the total of liabilities and equity is equal to the total of all assets at the close of each accounting period.
[S04322] To satisfy this requirement, all events that affect total assets and total liabilities unequally must eventually be reported as changes in equity.
[S04323] Businesses summarize their equity in a financial statement known as the balance sheet (or statement of net position) which shows the total assets, the specific equity balances, and the total liabilities and equity (or deficit).
[S04324] Various types of equity can appear on a balance sheet, depending on the form and purpose of the business entity.
[S04325] Preferred stock, share capital (or capital stock) and capital surplus (or additional paid-in capital) reflect original contributions to the business from its investors or organizers.
[S04326] Treasury stock appears as a contra-equity balance (an offset to equity) that reflects the amount that the business has paid to repurchase stock from shareholders.
[S04327] Retained earnings (or accumulated deficit) is the running total of the business's net income and losses, excluding any dividends.
[S04328] In the United Kingdom and other countries that use its accounting methods, equity includes various reserve accounts that are used for particular reconciliations of the balance sheet.
[S04329] Another financial statement, the statement of changes in equity, details the changes in these equity accounts from one accounting period to the next.
[S04330] Several events can produce changes in a firm's equity.
[S04331] * '''Capital investments''': Contributions of cash from outside the firm increase its base capital and capital surplus by the amount contributed.
[S04332] * '''Accumulated results''': Income or losses may be accumulated in an equity account called "retained earnings" or "accumulated deficit", depending on its net balance.
[S04333] * '''Unrealized investment results''': Changes in the value of securities that the firm owns, or foreign currency holdings, are accumulated in its equity.
[S04334] * '''Dividends''': The firm reduces its retained earnings by the amount of cash payable to shareholders.
[S04335] * '''Stock repurchases''': When the firm purchases shares into its own treasury, the amount paid for the stock is reflected in the treasury stock account.
[S04336] * '''Liquidation''': A firm that liquidates with positive equity can distribute it to owners in one or several cash payments.
[S04337] Equity investing is the business of purchasing stock in companies, either directly or from another investor, on the expectation that the stock will earn dividends or can be resold with a capital gain.
[S04338] Equity holders typically receive voting rights, meaning that they can vote on candidates for the board of directors and, if their holding is large enough, influence management decisions.
[S04339] Investors in a newly established firm must contribute an initial amount of capital to it so that it can begin to transact business.
[S04340] This contributed amount represents the investors' equity interest in the firm.
[S04341] In return, they receive shares of the company's stock.
[S04342] Under the model of a joint-stock company, the firm may keep contributed capital as long as it remains in business.
[S04343] If it liquidates, whether through a decision of the owners or through a bankruptcy process, the owners have a residual claim on the firm's eventual equity.
[S04344] If the equity is negative (a deficit) then the unpaid creditors bear loss and the owners' claim is void.
[S04345] Under limited liability, where the financial liability is limited to a fixed sum, owners are not required to pay the firm's debts themselves so long as the firm's books are in order and it has not involved the owners in fraud.
[S04346] When the owners of a firm are shareholders, their interest is called shareholders' equity.
[S04347] It is the difference between a company's assets and liabilities, and can be negative.
[S04348] If all shareholders are in one class, they share equally in ownership equity from all perspectives.
[S04349] It is not uncommon for companies to issue more than one class of stock, with each class having its own liquidation priority or voting rights.
[S04350] This complicates analysis for both stock valuation and accounting.
[S04351] A company's shareholder equity balance does not determine the price at which investors can sell its stock.
[S04352] Other relevant factors include the prospects and risks of its business, its access to necessary credit, and the difficulty of locating a buyer.
[S04353] According to the theory of intrinsic value, it is profitable to buy stock in a company when it is priced below the present value of the portion of its equity and future earnings that are payable to stockholders.
[S04354] Advocates of this method have included Benjamin Graham, Philip Fisher and Warren Buffett.
[S04355] An equity investment will never have a negative market value (i.e.
[S04356] become a liability) even if the firm has a shareholder deficit, because the deficit is not the owners' responsibility.
[S04357] An alternate approach, exemplified by the "Merton model", 
values stock-equity as a call option on the value of the whole company (including the liabilities), struck at the nominal value of the liabilities.
[S04358] The analogy with options arises in that limited liability protects equity investors:
(i) where the value of the firm is less than the value of the outstanding debt, shareholders may, and therefore would, choose not to repay the firm's debt;
(ii) where firm value is greater than debt value, the shareholders would choose to repay—i.e.
[S04359] exercise their option—and not to liquidate.
[S04360] Robert Merton, "Option Pricing When Underlying Stock Returns are Discontinuous" ''Journal of Financial Economics'', 3, January–March, 1976, pp.
[S04361] 125–44.
[S04362] -->

*Common ordinary equity
* Net worth
* Private equity

Category:Stock market

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SOURCE: Wikipedia — Debt
WORDS: 3,045
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
businesses lend money to customers, who then owe a debt to the payday loan company.]]
[S04363] '''Debt''' is an obligation that requires one party, the debtor, to pay money borrowed or otherwise withheld from another party, the creditor.
[S04364] Debt may be owed by a sovereign state or country, local government, company, or an individual.
[S04365] Commercial debt is generally subject to contractual terms regarding the amount and timing of repayments of principal and interest.
[S04366] Loans, bonds, notes, and mortgages are all types of debt.
[S04367] In financial accounting, debt is a type of financial transaction, as distinct from equity.
[S04368] The term can also be used metaphorically to cover moral obligations and other interactions not based on a monetary value.
[S04369] For example, in Western cultures, a person who has been helped by a second person is sometimes said to owe a "debt of gratitude" to the second person.
[S04370] The English term "debt" was first used in the late 13th century and comes by way of Old French from the Latin verb ''debere'', "to owe; to have from someone else."
[S04371] The related term "debtor" was first used in English also in the early 13th century.
[S04372] Principal is the amount of money originally invested or loaned, on which basis interest and returns are calculated.
[S04373] There are three main ways repayment may be structured: the entire principal balance may be due at the maturity of the loan; the entire principal balance may be amortized over the term of the loan; or the loan may be partially amortized during its term, with the remaining principal due as a "balloon payment" at maturity.
[S04374] Amortization structures are common in mortgages and credit cards.
[S04375] Debtors of every type default on their debt from time to time, with various consequences depending on the terms of the debt and the law governing default in the relevant jurisdiction.
[S04376] If the debt was secured by specific collateral, such as a car or house, the creditor may seek to repossess the collateral.
[S04377] In more serious circumstances, individuals and companies may go into bankruptcy.
[S04378] Common types of debt owed by individuals and households include mortgage loans, car loans, credit card debt, and income taxes.
[S04379] For individuals, debt is a means of using anticipated income and future purchasing power in the present before it has actually been earned.
[S04380] Commonly, people in industrialized nations use consumer debt to purchase houses, cars and other things too expensive to buy with cash on hand.
[S04381] People are likely to spend more and get into debt when they use credit cards as against cash to buy products and services.
[S04382] Chatterjee, P., & Rose, R. L. (2012).
[S04383] Do payment mechanisms change the way consumers perceive products?
[S04384] Journal of Consumer Research, 38(6), 1129–1139.
[S04385] Pettit, N. C., & Sivanathan, N. (2011).
[S04386] The plastic trap.
[S04387] Social Psychological and Personality Science, 2(2), 146-153.
[S04388] Prelec, D. & Loewenstein, G. (1998).
[S04389] The red and the black: Mental accounting of savings and debt.
[S04390] Marketing Science, 17(1), 4-28.
[S04391] Raghubir, P. & Srivastava, J.
[S04392] (2008), Monopoly money: The effect of payment coupling and form on spending behavior .
[S04393] Journal of Experimental Psychology: Applied, 14 (3), 213–25.
[S04394] This is primarily because of the transparency effect and consumer's "pain of paying."
[S04395] Soman, D. (2003).
[S04396] The effect of payment transparency on consumption: Quasi experiments from the field .
[S04397] Marketing Letters, 14, 173–183.
[S04398] The transparency effect refers to the idea that the further you are from cash (as with a credit card or other forms of payment), the less transparent it is and the less aware you are of how much you have spent.
[S04399] The less transparent or further away from cash the form of payment employed is, the less an individual feels the "pain of paying" and thus is likely to spend more.
[S04400] Furthermore, the differing physical appearance/form that credit cards have from cash may cause them to be viewed as "monopoly" money vs. real money, luring individuals to spend more money than they would if they only had cash available.
[S04401] Chatterjee, P., & Rose, R. L. (2012).
[S04402] Do payment mechanisms change the way consumers perceive products?
[S04403] Journal of Consumer Research, 38(6), 1129–1139.
[S04404] Besides these more formal debts, private individuals also lend informally to other people, mostly relatives or friends.
[S04405] One reason for such informal debts is that many people, in particular those who are poor, have no access to affordable credit.
[S04406] Such debts can cause problems when they are not paid back according to expectations of the lending household.
[S04407] In 2011, 8 percent of people in the European Union reported their households has been in arrears, that is, unable to pay as scheduled "payments related to informal loans from friends or relatives not living in your household".
[S04408] A company may use various kinds of debt to finance its operations as a part of its overall corporate finance strategy.
[S04409] A term loan is the simplest form of corporate debt.
[S04410] It consists of an agreement to lend a fixed amount of money, called the principal sum or principal, for a fixed period of time, with this amount to be repaid by a certain date.
[S04411] In commercial loans interest, calculated as a percentage of the principal sum per year, will also have to be paid by that date, or may be paid periodically in the interval, such as annually or monthly.
[S04412] Such loans are also colloquially called "bullet loans", particularly if there is only a single payment at the end – the "bullet" – without a "stream" of interest payments during the life of the loan.
[S04413] A revenue-based financing loan comes with a fixed repayment target that is reached over a period of several years.
[S04414] This type of loan generally comes with a repayment amount of 1.5 to 2.5 times the principle loan.
[S04415] Repayment periods are flexible; businesses can pay back the agreed-upon amount sooner, if possible, or later.
[S04416] In addition, business owners do not sell equity or relinquish control when using revenue-based financing.
[S04417] Lenders that provide revenue-based financing work more closely with businesses than bank lenders, but take a more hands-off approach than private equity investors.
[S04418] A syndicated loan is a loan that is granted to companies that wish to borrow more money than any single lender is prepared to risk in a single loan.
[S04419] A syndicated loan is provided by a group of lenders and is structured, arranged, and administered by one or several commercial banks or investment banks known as arrangers.
[S04420] Loan syndication is a risk management tool that allows the lead banks underwriting the debt to reduce their risk and free up lending capacity.
[S04421] A company may also issue bonds, which are debt securities.
[S04422] Bonds have a fixed lifetime, usually a number of years; with long-term bonds, lasting over 30 years, being less common.
[S04423] At the end of the bond's life the money should be repaid in full.
[S04424] Interest may be added to the end payment, or can be paid in regular installments (known as coupons) during the life of the bond.
[S04425] A letter of credit or LC can also be the source of payment for a transaction, meaning that redeeming the letter of credit will pay an exporter.
[S04426] Letters of credit are used primarily in international trade transactions of significant value, for deals between a supplier in one country and a customer in another.
[S04427] They are also used in the land development process to ensure that approved public facilities (streets, sidewalks, stormwater ponds, etc.)
[S04428] will be built.
[S04429] The parties to a letter of credit are usually a beneficiary who is to receive the money, the issuing bank of whom the applicant is a client, and the advising bank of whom the beneficiary is a client.
[S04430] Almost all letters of credit are irrevocable, i.e., cannot be amended or canceled without prior agreement of the beneficiary, the issuing bank and the confirming bank, if any.
[S04431] In executing a transaction, letters of credit incorporate functions common to giros and traveler's cheque.
[S04432] Typically, the documents a beneficiary has to present in order to receive payment include a commercial invoice, bill of lading, and a document proving the shipment was insured against loss or damage in transit.
[S04433] However, the list and form of documents is open to imagination and negotiation and might contain requirements to present documents issued by a neutral third party evidencing the quality of the goods shipped, or their place of origin.
[S04434] Debt consolidation is a process whereby a new, large loan application is submitted in order to compensate for numerous outstanding loans.
[S04435] Some amongst those who are heavily indebted often resort to debt consolidation as a means to resolve their financial difficulties.
[S04436] Upon obtaining the borrowed loan, those within the receiving end are then generally enabled to have a greater cash flow, resulting from lowering monthly payments, if not reducing interest rates.
[S04437] However, this varies from every claimant, in that their own eligibility for such is entirely dependent on their own overall circumstances; Should they meet specific requirements, being able to afford such, their requests are usually accepted; Should they fail the criteria, they're almost always swiftly rejected, regardless of their financial ability.
[S04438] Given the often monetary hardship of contenders, those providing these loans often charge at larger rates of interest than others; This is often critiqued by its opponents, who claim that it is an unfair practice aimed at targeting those who are desperate and often holds arbitrary figures, although those in its defence claim it is a security measure aimed at ensuring its repayment obligations and must take precautions before offering large sums.
[S04439] Both arguments have resulted in greater debate amongst legislators in different nations, amidst demands for further regulation and more decreases in lending restrictions.
[S04440] Debt consolidation has also been an area of interest for loan sharks, leaving those heavily indebted vulnerable to extortionate rates.
[S04441] The idea behind debt consolidation is occasionally a matter of debate in the financial and institutional sectors, often ranging between analysts towards professors, generally concerning ethics involved in different areas.
[S04442] Companies also use debt in many ways for capital expenditures and other business investments produced in their assets, "leveraging" the return on their equity.
[S04443] This leverage, the proportion of debt to equity, is considered paramount in determining the riskiness of an investment, under the notion that it becomes more risking under more debt.

]]
[S04444] Governments issue debt to pay for ongoing expenses as well as major capital projects.
[S04445] Government debt may be issued by sovereign states as well as by local governments, sometimes known as municipalities.
[S04446] Debt issued by the government of the United States, called Treasuries, serves as a reference point for all other debt.
[S04447] There are deep, transparent, liquid, and open capital markets for Treasuries.
[S04448] Lew, Jacob (2016), America and the Global Economy , Foreign Affairs, May/June 2016.
[S04449] Furthermore, Treasuries are issued in a wide variety of maturities, from one day to thirty years, which facilitates comparing the interest rates on other debt to a security of comparable maturity.
[S04450] In finance, the theoretical "risk-free interest rate" is often approximated by practitioners by using the current yield of a Treasury of the same duration.
[S04451] The overall level of indebtedness by a government is typically shown as a ratio of debt-to-GDP.
[S04452] This ratio helps to assess the speed of changes in government indebtedness and the size of the debt due.
[S04453] The United Nations Sustainable Development Goal 17, an integral part of the 2030 Agenda has a target to address the external debt of highly indebted poor countries to reduce debt distress.
[S04454] Municipal bonds (or muni bonds) are typical debt obligations, for which the conditions are defined unilaterally by the issuing municipality (local government), but it is a slower process to accumulate the necessary amount.
[S04455] Usually, debt or bond financing will not be used to finance current operating expenditures, the purposes of these amounts are local developments, capital investments, constructions, own contribution to other credits or grants.
[S04456] The debt service coverage ratio is the ratio of income available to the amount of debt service due (including both interest and principal amortization, if any).
[S04457] The higher the debt service coverage ratio, the more income is available to pay debt service, and the easier and lower-cost it will be for a borrower to obtain financing.
[S04458] Different debt markets have somewhat different conventions in terminology and calculations for income-related metrics.
[S04459] For example, in mortgage lending in the United States, a debt-to-income ratio typically includes the cost of mortgage payments as well as insurance and property tax, divided by a consumer's monthly income.
[S04460] A "front-end ratio" of 28% or below, together with a "back-end ratio" (including required payments on non-housing debt as well) of 36% or below is also required to be eligible for a conforming loan.
[S04461] The loan-to-value ratio is the ratio of the total amount of the loan to the total value of the collateral securing the loan.
[S04462] For example, in mortgage lending in the United States, the loan-to-value concept is most commonly expressed as a "down payment."
[S04463] A 20% down payment is equivalent to an 80% loan to value.
[S04464] With home purchases, value may be assessed using the agreed-upon purchase price, and/or an appraisal.
[S04465] A debt obligation is considered secured if creditors have recourse to specific collateral.
[S04466] Collateral may include claims on tax receipts (in the case of a government), specific assets (in the case of a company) or a home (in the case of a consumer).
[S04467] Unsecured debt comprises financial obligations for which creditors do not have recourse to the assets of the borrower to satisfy their claims.
[S04468] Credit bureaus collect information about the borrowing and repayment history of consumers.
[S04469] Lenders, such as banks and credit card companies, use credit scores to evaluate the potential risk posed by lending money to consumers.
[S04470] In the United States, the primary credit bureaus are Equifax, Experian, and TransUnion.
[S04471] Debts owed by governments and private corporations may be rated by rating agencies, such as Moody's, Standard & Poor's, Fitch Ratings, and A. M. Best.
[S04472] The government or company itself will also be given its own separate rating.
[S04473] These agencies assess the ability of the debtor to honor his obligations and accordingly give him or her a credit rating.
[S04474] Moody's uses the letters ''Aaa Aa A Baa Ba B Caa Ca C'', where ratings ''Aa-Caa'' are qualified by numbers 1–3.
[S04475] S&P and other rating agencies have slightly different systems using capital letters and +/- qualifiers.
[S04476] Thus a government or corporation with a high rating would have Aaa rating.
[S04477] A change in ratings can strongly affect a company, since its cost of refinancing depends on its creditworthiness.
[S04478] Bonds below Baa/BBB (Moody's/S&P) are considered junk or high-risk bonds.
[S04479] Their high risk of default (approximately 1.6 percent for Ba) is compensated by higher interest payments.
[S04480] Bad Debt is a loan that can not (partially or fully) be repaid by the debtor.
[S04481] The debtor is said to default on their debt.
[S04482] These types of debt are frequently repackaged and sold below face value.
[S04483] Buying junk bonds is seen as a risky but potentially profitable investment.
[S04484] Bonds are debt securities, tradeable on a bond market.
[S04485] A country's regulatory structure determines what qualifies as a security.
[S04486] For example, in North America, each security is uniquely identified by a CUSIP for trading and settlement purposes.
[S04487] In contrast, loans are not securities and do not have CUSIPs (or the equivalent).
[S04488] Loans may be sold or acquired in certain circumstances, as when a bank syndicates a loan.
[S04489] Loans can be turned into securities through the securitization process.
[S04490] In a securitization, a company sells a pool of assets to a securitization trust, and the securitization trust finances its purchase of the assets by selling securities to the market.
[S04491] For example, a trust may own a pool of home mortgages, and be financed by residential mortgage-backed securities.
[S04492] In this case, the asset-backed trust is a debt issuer of residential mortgage-backed securities.
[S04493] Central banks, such as the U.S. Federal Reserve System, play a key role in the debt markets.
[S04494] Debt is normally denominated in a particular currency, and so changes in the valuation of that currency can change the effective size of the debt.
[S04495] This can happen due to inflation or deflation, so it can happen even though the borrower and the lender are using the same currency.
[S04496] At the household level, debts can also have detrimental effects — particularly when households make spending decisions assuming income will increase, or remain stable, in years to come.
[S04497] When households take on credit based on this assumption, life events can easily change indebtedness into over-indebtedness.
[S04498] Such life events include unexpected unemployment, relationship break-up, leaving the parental home, business failure, illness, or home repairs.
[S04499] Over-indebtedness has severe social consequences, such as financial hardship, poor physical and mental health, family stress, stigma, difficulty obtaining employment, exclusion from basic financial services (European Commission, 2009), work accidents and industrial disease, a strain on social relations (Carpentier and Van den Bosch, 2008), absenteeism at work and lack of organisational commitment (Kim ''et al.
[S04500] '', 2003), feeling of insecurity, and relational tensions.
[S04501] According to historian Paul Johnson, the lending of "food money" was commonplace in Middle Eastern civilizations as early as 5000 BC.
[S04502] Religions like Judaism and Christianity for example, demand that debt be forgiven on a regular basis, in order to prevent systemic inequities between groups in society, or anyone becoming a specialist in holding debt and coercing repayment.
[S04503] An example is the Biblical Jubilee year, described in the Book of Leviticus.
[S04504] Similarly, in Deuteronomy chapter 15 and verse 1 states that debts be forgiven after seven years.
[S04505] This is because biblically debt is seen as the responsibility of both the creditor and the debtor.
[S04506] Traditional Christian teaching holds that a lifestyle of debt should not be normative; the Emmanuel Association, a Methodist denomination in the conservative holiness movement, for example, teaches: "We are to refrain from entering into debt when we have no reasonable plan to pay.
[S04507] We are to be careful to meet all financial engagements promptly when due, if at all possible, remembering that we are to 'Provide things honest in the sight of all men' and to 'owe no man any thing, but to love one another' (Romans 12:17; 13:8)."
[S04508] * World Bank, 2019.
[S04509] ''Global Waves of Debt: Causes and Consequences''.
[S04510] Edited by M. Ayhan Kose, Peter Nagle, Franziska Ohnsorge, and Naotaka Sugawara.
[S04511] * Debt theory of money
* Debt deflation
* Negative equity
* World debt

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SOURCE: Wikipedia — Credit rating
WORDS: 1,115
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
A '''credit rating''' is an evaluation of the credit risk of a prospective debtor (an individual, a business, company or a government).
[S04512] It is the practice of predicting or forecasting the ability of a supposed debtor to pay back the debt or default.
[S04513] The credit rating represents an evaluation from a credit rating agency of the qualitative and quantitative information for the prospective debtor, including information provided by the prospective debtor and other non-public information obtained by the credit rating agency's analysts.
[S04514] Credit reporting (or credit score) is a subset of credit rating.
[S04515] It is a numeric evaluation of an ''individual's'' credit worthiness, which is done by a credit bureau or consumer credit reporting agency.
[S04516] A sovereign credit rating is the credit rating of a sovereign entity, such as a national government.
[S04517] The sovereign credit rating indicates the risk level of the investing environment of a country and is used by investors when looking to invest in particular jurisdictions, and also takes into account political risk.
[S04518] The "country risk rankings" table shows the ten least-risky countries for investment .
[S04519] Ratings are further broken down into components including political risk, economic risk.
[S04520] Euromoney's bi-annual country risk index monitors the political and economic stability of 185 sovereign countries, with Singapore often emerging as the least risky country since 2017 – it is also one of the only few countries in the world as well as the only in Asia to achieve a AAA sovereign credit rankings from all major credit agencies.
[S04521] Results focus foremost on economics, specifically sovereign default risk or payment default risk for exporters (also known as a trade credit risk).
[S04522] A. M. Best defines "country risk" as the risk that country-specific factors could adversely affect an insurer's ability to meet its financial obligations.
[S04523] A rating expresses the likelihood that the rated party will go into default within a given time horizon.
[S04524] In general, a time horizon of one year or under is considered short term, and anything above that is considered long term.
[S04525] In the past institutional investors preferred to consider long-term ratings.
[S04526] Nowadays, short-term ratings are commonly used.
[S04527] Credit ratings can address a corporation's financial instruments i.e.
[S04528] debt security such as a bond, but also the corporations itself.
[S04529] Ratings are assigned by credit rating agencies, the largest of which are Standard & Poor's, Moody's and Fitch Ratings.
[S04530] They use letter designations such as A, B, C. Higher grades are intended to represent a lower probability of default.
[S04531] Agencies do not attach a hard number of probability of default to each grade, preferring descriptive definitions such as: "the obligor's capacity to meet its financial commitment on the obligation is extremely strong," or "less vulnerable to non-payment than other speculative issues…" (Standard and Poors' definition of an AAA-rated and a BB-rated bond respectively).
[S04532] However, some studies have estimated the average risk and reward of bonds by rating.
[S04533] One study by Moody's Cantor, R., Hamilton, D.T., Kim, F., and Ou, S., 2007 Corporate default and recovery rates.
[S04534] 1920–2006, ''Special Comment:'' Moody's investor Service, June Report 102071, 1-48 page 24 cited by authors Herwig Langohr and Patricia Langohr claimed that over a "5-year time horizon" bonds it gave its highest rating (Aaa) to had a "cumulative default rate" of 0.18%, the next highest (Aa2) 0.28%, the next (Baa2) 2.11%, 8.82% for the next (Ba2), and 31.24% for the lowest it studied (B2).
[S04535] (See "Default rate" in "Estimated spreads and default rates by rating grade" table to right.)
[S04536] Over a longer period, it stated "the order is by and large, but not exactly, preserved".
[S04537] Another study in ''Journal of Finance'' calculated the additional interest rate or "spread" corporate bonds pay over that of "riskless" US Treasury bonds, according to the bonds' rating.
[S04538] (See "Basis point spread" in table to right.)
[S04539] Looking at rated bonds for 1973–89, the authors found a AAA-rated bond paid 43 "basis points" (or 43/100 of a percentage point) over a US Treasury bond (so that it would yield 3.43% if the Treasury yielded 3.00%).
[S04540] A CCC-rated "junk" (or speculative) bond, on the other hand, paid over 7% (724 basis points) more than a Treasury bond on average over that period.
[S04541] from Altman, Edward I "Measuring Corporate Bond Mortality and Performance" ''Journal of Finance'', (September 1989) pp.
[S04542] 909–22 

Different rating agencies may use variations of an alphabetical combination of lowercase and uppercase letters, with either plus or minus signs or numbers added to further fine-tune the rating (see colored chart).
[S04543] The Standard & Poor's rating scale uses uppercase letters and pluses and minuses.
[S04544] The Moody's rating system uses numbers and lowercase letters as well as uppercase.
[S04545] While Moody's, S&P and Fitch Ratings control approximately 95% of the credit ratings business, they are not the only rating agencies.
[S04546] DBRS's long-term ratings scale is somewhat similar to Standard & Poor's and Fitch Ratings with the words high and low replacing the + and −.
[S04547] It goes as follows, from excellent to poor: AAA, AA (high), AA, AA (low), A (high), A, A (low), BBB (high), BBB, BBB (low), BB (high), BB, BB (low), B (high), B, B (low), CCC (high), CCC, CCC (low), CC (high), CC, CC (low), C (high), C, C (low) and D. The short-term ratings often map to long-term ratings though there is room for exceptions at the high or low side of each equivalent.
[S04548] S&P, Moody's, Fitch and DBRS are the only four ratings agencies that are recognized by the European Central Bank (ECB) for determining collateral requirements for banks to borrow from the central bank.
[S04549] The ECB uses a first, best rule among the four agencies that have the designated ECAI status, which means that it takes the highest rating among the four agencies – S&P, Moody's, Fitch and DBRS – to determine haircuts and collateral requirements for borrowing.
[S04550] Ratings in Europe have been under close scrutiny, particularly the highest ratings given to countries like Spain, Ireland and Italy, because they affect how much banks can borrow against sovereign debt they hold.
[S04551] A. M. Best rates from excellent to poor in the following manner: A++, A+, A, A−, B++, B+, B, B−, C++, C+, C, C−, D, E, F, and S. The CTRISKS rating system is as follows: CT3A, CT2A, CT1A, CT3B, CT2B, CT1B, CT3C, CT2C and CT1C.
[S04552] All these CTRISKS grades are mapped to one-year probability of default.
[S04553] Under the EU Credit Rating Agency Regulation (CRAR), the European Banking Authority has developed a series of mapping tables that map ratings to the "Credit Quality Steps" (CQS) as set out in regulatory capital rules and map the CQS to short run and long run benchmark default rates.
[S04554] These are provided in the table below:

* 
*Singapore Credit Score Guide
