[S06623] ", ''The New York Review of Books'', vol.LXXII, no.
[S06624] 20 (18 December 2025), pp.&nbsp;61–62.
[S06625] "Cryptocurrency ... has often become a vehicle for fraud and criminality.
[S06626] ...
[S06627] The only way a cryptocurrency investor could make money was by selling his tokens when their price rose.
[S06628] And such selling, untethered to any underlying economic reality, could cause the price of Bitcoin to fall rapidly, as it did ... in 2022, leading to billions of dollars in losses."
[S06629] (p.&nbsp;61.)
[S06630] * 
* 
* 

 
Category:Applications of cryptography
Category:Decentralization
Category:Financial technology

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SOURCE: Wikipedia — Bitcoin
WORDS: 4,269
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
'''Bitcoin''' (abbreviation: '''BTC'''; sign: '''₿''') is the first decentralized cryptocurrency.
[S06631] Based on a free-market ideology, bitcoin was invented in 2008 when an unknown person published a white paper under the pseudonym of Satoshi Nakamoto.
[S06632] Use of bitcoin as a currency began in 2009, with the release of its open-source implementation.
[S06633] From 2021 to 2025, El Salvador adopted it as legal tender currency before revoking it.
[S06634] As bitcoin is pseudonymous, its use by criminals has attracted the attention of regulators, leading to its ban by several countries.
[S06635] Bitcoin works through the collaboration of computers, each of which acts as a node in the peer-to-peer bitcoin network.
[S06636] Each node maintains an independent copy of a public distributed ledger of transactions, called a blockchain, without central oversight.
[S06637] Transactions are validated through the use of cryptography, preventing one person from spending another person's bitcoin, as long as the owner of the bitcoin keeps certain sensitive data secret.
[S06638] Consensus between nodes about the content of the blockchain is achieved using a computationally intensive process based on proof of work, called mining, which is performed by purpose-built computers.
[S06639] Mining consumes large quantities of electricity and has been criticized for its environmental impact.
[S06640] Before bitcoin, several digital cash technologies were released, starting with David Chaum's ecash in the 1980s.
[S06641] The idea that solutions to computational puzzles could have some value was first proposed by cryptographers Cynthia Dwork and Moni Naor in 1992.
[S06642] The concept was independently rediscovered by Adam Back who developed Hashcash, a proof-of-work scheme for spam control in 1997.
[S06643] The first proposals for distributed digital scarcity-based cryptocurrencies came from cypherpunks Wei Dai (b-money) and Nick Szabo (bit gold) in 1998.
[S06644] In 2004, Hal Finney developed the first currency based on reusable proof of work.
[S06645] These various attempts were not successful: Chaum's concept required centralized control and no banks wanted to sign on, Hashcash had no protection against double-spending, while b-money and bit gold were not resistant to Sybil attacks.
[S06646] The domain name ''bitcoin.org'' was registered on 18 August 2008.
[S06647] On 31 October 2008, a link to a white paper authored by Satoshi Nakamoto titled ''Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System'' was posted to a cryptography mailing list.
[S06648] Nakamoto's identity remains unknown.
[S06649] According to computer scientist Arvind Narayanan, all individual components of bitcoin originated in earlier academic literature.
[S06650] Nakamoto's innovation was their complex interplay resulting in the first decentralized, Sybil resistant, Byzantine fault tolerant digital cash system, that would eventually be referred to as the first blockchain.
[S06651] Nakamoto's paper was not peer reviewed and was initially ignored by academics, who argued that it could not work.
[S06652] Nakamoto released bitcoin as open-source software.
[S06653] On 3 January 2009, the bitcoin network was created when Nakamoto mined the starting block of the chain, known as the ''genesis block''.
[S06654] Embedded in this block was the text "The Times 03/Jan/2009 Chancellor on brink of second bailout for banks", which is the date and headline of an issue of ''The Times'' newspaper.
[S06655] Nine days later, Hal Finney received the first bitcoin transaction: ten bitcoins from Nakamoto.
[S06656] Wei Dai and Nick Szabo were also early supporters.
[S06657] On May 22, 2010, the first known commercial transaction using bitcoin occurred when programmer Laszlo Hanyecz bought two Papa John's pizzas for ₿10,000, in what would later be celebrated as "Bitcoin Pizza Day".
[S06658] Satoshi tasked Finnish developer and early Bitcoin contributor with creating content for the bitcoin.org website.
[S06659] Blockchain analysts estimate that Nakamoto had mined about one million bitcoins before disappearing in 2010 when he handed the network alert key and control of the code repository over to Gavin Andresen.
[S06660] Andresen later became lead developer at the Bitcoin Foundation, an organization founded in September 2012 to promote bitcoin.
[S06661] After early "proof-of-concept" transactions, the first major users of bitcoin were black markets, such as the dark web Silk Road.
[S06662] During its 30 months of existence, beginning in February 2011, Silk Road exclusively accepted bitcoins as payment, transacting ₿9.9&nbsp;million, worth about $214&nbsp;million.
[S06663] In March 2013, the US Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) established regulatory guidelines for "decentralized virtual currencies" such as bitcoin, classifying American bitcoin miners who sell their generated bitcoins as money services businesses, subject to registration and other legal obligations.
[S06664] In May 2013, US authorities seized the unregistered exchange Mt.
[S06665] Gox.
[S06666] In June 2013, the US Drug Enforcement Administration seized ₿11.02 from an individual attempting to use them to purchase illicit drugs.
[S06667] This marked the first time a government agency had seized bitcoins.
[S06668] The FBI seized about ₿30,000 in October 2013 from Silk Road, following the arrest of its founder Ross Ulbricht.
[S06669] In December 2013, the People's Bank of China prohibited Chinese financial institutions from using bitcoin.
[S06670] After the announcement, the value of bitcoin dropped, and Baidu no longer accepted bitcoins for certain services.
[S06671] Buying real-world goods with any virtual currency had been illegal in China since at least 2009.
[S06672] Research produced by the University of Cambridge estimated that in 2017, there were 2.9 to 5.8 million unique users using a cryptocurrency wallet, most of them using bitcoin.
[S06673] In August 2017, the SegWit software upgrade was activated.
[S06674] Segwit was intended to support the Lightning Network as well as improve scalability.
[S06675] SegWit opponents, who supported larger blocks as a scalability solution, forked to create Bitcoin Cash, one of many forks of bitcoin.
[S06676] In December 2017, the first futures on bitcoin was introduced by the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME).
[S06677] In February 2018, the price crashed after China imposed a complete ban on bitcoin trading.
[S06678] The percentage of bitcoin trading in the Chinese renminbi fell from over 90% in September 2017 to less than 1% in June 2018.
[S06679] During the same year, bitcoin prices were negatively affected by several hacks or thefts from cryptocurrency exchanges.
[S06680] |date=4 December 2024 |url=https://fred.stlouisfed.org/graph/?g=1BVJt |access-date=4 December 2024 |publisher=Federal Reserve Economic Data}} }} December 1, 2014 – December 4, 2024]]
In 2020, some major companies and institutions started to acquire bitcoin: MicroStrategy invested $250 million in bitcoin as a treasury reserve asset, Square, Inc., $50 million, and MassMutual, $100 million.
[S06681] In November 2020, PayPal added support for bitcoin in the US.
[S06682] In February 2021, bitcoin's market capitalization reached $1 trillion for the first time.
[S06683] In November 2021, the Taproot soft-fork upgrade was activated, adding support for Schnorr signatures, improved functionality of smart contracts and Lightning Network.
[S06684] Before, bitcoin only used a custom elliptic curve with the ECDSA algorithm to produce signatures.
[S06685] In September 2021, bitcoin became legal tender currency in El Salvador, alongside the US dollar.
[S06686] In October 2021, the first bitcoin futures exchange-traded fund (ETF), called BITO, from ProShares was approved by the SEC and listed on the CME.
[S06687] In early 2022, during the Canadian trucker protests opposing COVID-19 vaccine mandates, organizers turned to bitcoin to receive donations after traditional financial platforms restricted access to funding.
[S06688] Proponents highlighted bitcoin's use as a tool for fundraising in situations where access to conventional financial systems may be restricted.
[S06689] In May and June 2022, the bitcoin price fell following the collapses of TerraUSD, a stablecoin, and the Celsius Network, a cryptocurrency loan company.
[S06690] In 2023, ordinals—non-fungible tokens (NFTs)—on bitcoin, went live.
[S06691] As of June 2023, River Financial estimated that bitcoin had 81.7 million users, about 1% of the global population.
[S06692] In January 2024, the first 11 US spot bitcoin ETFs began trading, offering direct exposure to bitcoin for the first time on American stock exchanges.
[S06693] In December 2024, bitcoin price reached $100,000 for the first time, as US president-elect Donald Trump promised to make the US the "crypto capital of the planet" and to stockpile bitcoin.
[S06694] The same month, BlackRock, the world's largest asset manager, recommended investors to allocate up to 2% of their portfolio to bitcoin.
[S06695] As of 2025, the Salvadoran government continues to describe Bitcoin as legal tender under the 2021 Bitcoin Law; however, a January 2025 reform removed obligations for businesses and the government to accept it, so many analysts regard its legal-tender status as effectively ended in practice.
[S06696] In March 2025, President Trump signed an executive order to establish a strategic bitcoin reserve.
[S06697] Later that year, some U.S. states, such as Texas and New Hampshire also instituted strategic bitcoin reserves, and the Czech National Bank bought less than €1 million in a "test portfolio".
[S06698] The unit of account of the bitcoin system is the ''bitcoin''.
[S06699] It is most commonly represented with the symbol ₿ designed in 2010 and the currency code BTC.
[S06700] However, the BTC code does not conform to ISO 4217 as BT is the country code of Bhutan, and ISO 4217 requires the first letter used in global commodities to be 'X'.
[S06701] XBT, a code that conforms to ISO 4217 though not officially part of it, is used by Bloomberg L.P. 

No uniform capitalization convention exists; some sources use ''Bitcoin'', capitalized, to refer to the technology and network, and ''bitcoin'', lowercase, for the unit of account.
[S06702] The ''Cambridge Advanced Learner's Dictionary'' and the ''Oxford Advanced Learner's Dictionary'' use the capitalized and lowercase variants without distinction.
[S06703] Bitcoin, ''Oxford Advanced Learner's Dictionary'' bitcoin , ''Cambridge Advanced Learner's Dictionary'' 

One bitcoin is divisible to eight decimal places.
[S06704] Units for smaller amounts of bitcoin are the millibitcoin (mBTC), equal to bitcoin, and the satoshi (sat), representing (one hundred millionth) bitcoin, the smallest amount possible.
[S06705] 100,000 satoshis are one mBTC.
[S06706] As a decentralized system, bitcoin operates without a central authority or single administrator, so that anyone can create a new bitcoin address and transact without needing any approval.
[S06707] This is accomplished through a specialized distributed ledger called a ''blockchain'' that records bitcoin transactions.
[S06708] The blockchain is implemented as an ordered list of ''blocks''.
[S06709] Each block contains a SHA-256 hash of the previous block, chaining them in chronological order.
[S06710] The blockchain is maintained by a peer-to-peer network.
[S06711] Individual blocks, public addresses, and transactions within blocks are public information, and can be examined using a blockchain explorer.
[S06712] Nodes validate and broadcast transactions, each maintaining a copy of the blockchain for ownership verification.
[S06713] A new block is created every 10 minutes on average, updating the blockchain across all nodes without central oversight.
[S06714] This process tracks bitcoin spending, ensuring each bitcoin is spent only once.
[S06715] Unlike a traditional ledger that tracks physical currency, bitcoins exist digitally as unspent outputs of transactions.
[S06716] In the blockchain, bitcoins are linked to specific strings called addresses.
[S06717] Most often, an address encodes a hash of a single public key.
[S06718] Creating such an address involves generating a random private key and then computing the corresponding address.
[S06719] This process is almost instant, but the reverse (finding the private key for a given address) is nearly impossible.
[S06720] Publishing such a bitcoin address does not risk its private key, and it is extremely unlikely to accidentally generate a used key with funds.
[S06721] To use bitcoins, owners need their private key to digitally sign transactions, which are verified by the network using the public key, keeping the private key secret.
[S06722] An address may encode the hash of a bitcoin script that specifies more complex requirements to spend the funds.
[S06723] One common example is "multisig", in which multiple distinct private keys must mutually sign any transaction that attempts to spend the funds.
[S06724] Bitcoin transactions use a Forth-like scripting language, involving one or more inputs and outputs.
[S06725] When sending bitcoins, a user specifies the recipients' addresses and the amount for each output.
[S06726] This allows sending bitcoins to several recipients in a single transaction.
[S06727] To prevent double-spending, each input must refer to a previous unspent output in the blockchain.
[S06728] Using multiple inputs is similar to using multiple coins in a cash transaction.
[S06729] As in a cash transaction, the sum of inputs can exceed the intended sum of payments.
[S06730] In such a case, an additional output can return the change back to the payer.
[S06731] Unallocated input satoshis in the transaction become the transaction fee.
[S06732] Losing a private key means losing access to the bitcoins, with no other proof of ownership accepted by the protocol.
[S06733] For instance, in 2013, a user lost ₿7,500, valued at US$7.5 million, by accidentally discarding a hard drive with the private key.
[S06734] It is estimated that around 20% of all bitcoins are lost.
[S06735] The private key must also be kept secret as its exposure, such as through a data breach, can lead to theft of the associated bitcoins.
[S06736] , approximately ₿980,000 had been stolen from cryptocurrency exchanges.
[S06737] with large amounts of mining hardware]]
Miners don't directly act as nodes, but do communicate with nodes.
[S06738] The mining process is primarily intended to prevent double-spending and get all nodes to agree on the content of the blockchain, but it also has desirable side-effects such as making it infeasible for adversaries to stifle valid transactions or alter the historical record of transactions, since doing so generally requires the adversary to have access to more mining power than the rest of the network combined.
[S06739] The mining process in bitcoin involves maintaining the blockchain through computer processing power.
[S06740] Miners group and broadcast new transactions into blocks, which are then verified by the network.
[S06741] Each block must contain a proof of work (PoW) to be accepted, involving finding a nonce number that, combined with the block content, produces a hash numerically smaller than the network's ''difficulty target''.
[S06742] This PoW is simple to verify but hard to generate, requiring many attempts.
[S06743] PoW forms the basis of bitcoin's consensus mechanism.
[S06744] The difficulty of generating a block is deterministically adjusted based on the mining power on the network by changing the difficulty target, which is recalibrated every 2,016 blocks (approximately two weeks) to maintain an average time of ten minutes between new blocks.
[S06745] The process requires significant computational power and specialized hardware.
[S06746] Miners who successfully create a new block with a valid nonce can collect transaction fees from the included transactions and a fixed reward in bitcoins.
[S06747] To claim this reward, a special transaction called a ''coinbase'' is included in the block, with the miner as the payee.
[S06748] All bitcoins in existence have been created through this type of transaction.
[S06749] This reward is halved every 210,000 blocks until ₿21&nbsp;million have been issued in total, which is expected to occur around the year 2140.
[S06750] Afterward, miners will only earn from transaction fees.
[S06751] These fees are determined by the transaction's size and the amount of data stored, measured in satoshis per byte.
[S06752] The proof of work system and the chaining of blocks make blockchain modifications very difficult, as altering one block requires changing all subsequent blocks.
[S06753] As more blocks are added, modifying older blocks becomes increasingly challenging.
[S06754] In case of disagreement, nodes trust the longest chain, which required the greatest amount of effort to produce.
[S06755] To tamper or censor the ledger, one needs to control the majority of the global hashrate.
[S06756] The high cost required to reach this level of computational power secures the bitcoin blockchain.
[S06757] The environmental impact of bitcoin mining is controversial and has attracted the attention of regulators, leading to restrictions or incentives in various jurisdictions.
[S06758] , a non-peer-reviewed study by the Cambridge Centre for Alternative Finance (CCAF) estimated that bitcoin mining represented % of global electricity consumption and % of world greenhouse gas emissions, comparable to Slovakia's emissions.
[S06759] About half of the electricity used is generated through fossil fuels.
[S06760] Moreover, mining hardware's short lifespan results in electronic waste.
[S06761] In October 2025, the US was the largest mining country with 38% of the global bitcoin mining market share, followed by Russia (16%) and China (14%, despite all cryptocurrency trading and mining being banned there in 2021).
[S06762] Bitcoin is pseudonymous, with funds linked to addresses, not real-world identities.
[S06763] While the owners of these addresses are not directly identified, all transactions are public on the blockchain.
[S06764] Patterns of use, like spending coins from multiple inputs, can hint at a common owner.
[S06765] Public data can sometimes be matched with known address owners.
[S06766] Bitcoin exchanges might also need to collect personal data as per legal requirements.
[S06767] For enhanced privacy, users can generate a new address for each transaction.
[S06768] In the bitcoin network, each bitcoin is treated equally, ensuring basic fungibility.
[S06769] However, users and applications can choose to differentiate between bitcoins.
[S06770] While wallets and software treat all bitcoins the same, each bitcoin's transaction history is recorded on the blockchain.
[S06771] This public record allows for chain analysis, where users can identify and potentially reject bitcoins from controversial sources.
[S06772] For example, in 2012, Mt.
[S06773] Gox froze accounts containing bitcoins identified as stolen.
[S06774] Bitcoin wallets were the first cryptocurrency wallets, enabling users to store the information necessary to transact bitcoins.
[S06775] The first wallet program, simply named ''Bitcoin'', and sometimes referred to as the ''Satoshi client'', was released in 2009 by Nakamoto as open-source software.
[S06776] Bitcoin Core is among the best known clients.
[S06777] Forks of Bitcoin Core exist such as Bitcoin Unlimited.
[S06778] Wallets can be full clients, with a full copy of the blockchain to check the validity of mined blocks, or lightweight clients, just to send and receive transactions without a local copy of the entire blockchain.
[S06779] Third-party internet services, called online wallets or hot wallets, store users' credentials on their servers, making them susceptible of hacks.
[S06780] Cold storage protects bitcoins from such hacks by keeping private keys offline, either through specialized hardware wallets or paper printouts.
[S06781] Nakamoto limited the block size to one megabyte.
[S06782] The limited block size and frequency can lead to delayed processing of transactions, increased fees and a bitcoin scalability problem.
[S06783] The Lightning Network, a second-layer routing network, is a potential scaling solution.
[S06784] Research shows a trend towards centralization in bitcoin as miners join pools for stable income.
[S06785] If a single miner or pool controls more than 50% of the hashing power, it would allow them to censor transactions and double-spend coins.
[S06786] In 2014, mining pool Ghash.io reached 51% mining power, causing safety concerns, but later voluntarily capped its power at 39.99% for the benefit of the whole network.
[S06787] A few entities also dominate other parts of the ecosystem such as the client software, online wallets, and simplified payment verification (SPV) clients.
[S06788] According to the European Central Bank, the decentralization of money offered by bitcoin has its theoretical roots in the Austrian school of economics, especially with Friedrich Hayek's ''The Denationalisation of Money'', in which he advocates a complete free market in the production, distribution and management of money to end the monopoly of central banks.
[S06789] Sociologist Nigel Dodd argues that the essence of the bitcoin ideology is to remove money from social, as well as governmental, control.
[S06790] ''The Economist'' describes bitcoin as "a techno-anarchist project to create an online version of cash, a way for people to transact without the possibility of interference from malicious governments or banks".
[S06791] These philosophical ideas initially attracted libertarians and anarchists.
[S06792] Money serves three purposes: a store of value, a medium of exchange, and a unit of account.
[S06793] According to ''The Economist'' in 2014, bitcoin functions best as a medium of exchange.
[S06794] In 2015, ''The Economist'' noted that bitcoins had three qualities useful in a currency: they are "hard to earn, limited in supply and easy to verify".
[S06795] However, a 2018 assessment by ''The Economist'' stated that cryptocurrencies met none of these three criteria.
[S06796] Per some researchers, , bitcoin functions more as a payment system than as a currency.
[S06797] In 2014, economist Robert J. Shiller wrote that bitcoin has potential as a unit of account for measuring the relative value of goods, as with Chile's Unidad de Fomento, but that "Bitcoin in its present form... doesn't really solve any sensible economic problem".
[S06798] In 2017, François Velde, senior economist at the Chicago Fed, described bitcoin as "unlikely by itself to replace monies in well-functioning monetary systems."
[S06799] The legal status of bitcoin varies substantially from one jurisdiction to another.
[S06800] Because of its decentralized nature and its global presence, regulating bitcoin is difficult.
[S06801] However, the use of bitcoin can be criminalized, and shutting down exchanges and the peer-to-peer economy in a given country would constitute a de facto ban.
[S06802] The use of bitcoin by criminals has attracted the attention of financial regulators, legislative bodies, and law enforcement.
[S06803] Nobel-prize winning economist Joseph Stiglitz says that bitcoin's anonymity encourages money laundering and other crimes.
[S06804] This is the main justification behind bitcoin bans.
[S06805] , nine countries applied an absolute ban (Algeria, Bangladesh, China, Egypt, Iraq, Morocco, Nepal, Qatar, and Tunisia) while another 42 countries had an implicit ban.
[S06806] accepting Bitcoin]]
According to Harvard Professor Kenneth Rogoff , bitcoin is rarely used in regular transactions with merchants, but is popular in the informal economy and for criminal activities.
[S06807] Ken Rogoff on How Crypto Is Cutting Into the Dollar’s Hegemony, Vildana Hajric, Bloomberg, May 24, 2025 Prices are not usually quoted in bitcoin and trades involve conversions into fiat currencies.
[S06808] Commonly cited reasons for not using bitcoin include high costs, the inability to process chargebacks, high price volatility, long transaction times, and transaction fees (especially for small purchases).
[S06809] ''Bloomberg'' reported that bitcoin was being used for large-item purchases on the site Overstock.com and for cross-border payments to freelancers.
[S06810] , there was little sign of bitcoin use in international remittances despite high fees charged by banks and Western Union.
[S06811] From September 2021 until January 2025, the Bitcoin Law made bitcoin a legal tender currency in El Salvador, alongside the US dollar.
[S06812] The adoption had been criticized internationally and within El Salvador.
[S06813] In 2022, the International Monetary Fund (IMF) urged El Salvador to reverse its decision.
[S06814] , the use of Bitcoin in El Salvador remained low: 80% of businesses refused to accept it.
[S06815] In 2025, El Salvador's government revoked bitcoin's status as legal tender currency in order to comply with conditions set by the IMF for a loan.
[S06816] El Salvador still describes bitcoin as "legal tender", but its acceptance is no longer obligitory (as it is with the US dollar) and the El Salvador government no longer accepts bitcoin for payment of taxes or fees.
[S06817] In April 2022, the Central African Republic (CAR) adopted bitcoin as legal tender alongside the CFA franc, but repealed the reform one year later.
[S06818] Bitcoin is also used by some governments.
[S06819] For instance, the Iranian government initially opposed cryptocurrencies, but later began using them to circumvent sanctions.
[S06820] Since 2020, Iran has required local bitcoin miners to sell bitcoin to the Central Bank of Iran, allowing the central bank to use it for imports.
[S06821] Some constituent states and local governments also accept tax payments in bitcoin, including Colorado in the US and Zug and Lugano in Switzerland.
[S06822] As of 2023, the US government owned more than $5 billion worth of seized bitcoin.
[S06823] , the overwhelming majority of bitcoin transactions took place on cryptocurrency exchanges.
[S06824] Since 2014, regulated bitcoin funds also allow exposure to the asset or to futures as an investment.
[S06825] Bitcoin is used as a store of value: individuals and companies such as the Winklevoss twins and Elon Musk's companies SpaceX and Tesla have each bought and sold hundreds of millions of dollars worth of bitcoin.
[S06826] Bitcoin wealth is highly concentrated, with 0.01% holding 27% of in-circulation currency, as of 2021.
[S06827] As of March 2025, El Salvador had $550 million worth of bitcoin in its international reserves, about 6,102 coins.
[S06828] Bitcoin, along with other cryptocurrencies, has been described as an economic bubble by several economists, including Nobel Prize in Economics laureates, such as Joseph Stiglitz, James Heckman, and Paul Krugman.
[S06829] Another recipient of the prize, Robert Shiller, argues that bitcoin is rather a fad that may become an asset class.
[S06830] He describes its price growth as an "epidemic", driven by contagious narratives.
[S06831] In 2024, Jean Tirole, also Nobel laureate, described bitcoin as a "pure bubble" as its intrinsic value is zero.
[S06832] According to him, some bubbles are long-lasting such as gold and fiat currencies, and it is impossible to predict whether bitcoin would implode like other financial bubbles or become an alternative to gold.
[S06833] The same year, Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell described bitcoin as a digital competitor to gold but not to the dollar as he argued it is a highly volatile speculative asset not used as a form of payment.
[S06834] In 2025, Kenneth Rogoff claimed that Krugman was wrong and that Bitcoin had value as it is competing with the dollar to become the means of exchange of the underground economy which represents 20% of the world's GDP.
[S06835] According to Rogoff, bitcoin is "the ideal currency for a more fragmented and uncertain global landscape" and it could become appealing to emerging-market central banks as a "politically neutral" reserve currency.
[S06836] In 2025, the nominee for the Chair of the Federal Reserve, Kevin Warsh, described Bitcoin as the "new gold."
[S06837] According to research published in the ''International Review of Financial Analysis'' in 2018, bitcoin as an asset is highly volatile and does not behave like any other conventional asset.
[S06838] According to one 2022 analysis published in ''The Journal of Alternative Investments'', bitcoin was less volatile than oil, silver, US Treasuries, and 190 stocks in the S&P 500 during and after the 2020 stock market crash.
[S06839] The term was created in December 2013 for holding bitcoin rather than selling it during periods of volatility.
[S06840] In 2014, economist Nouriel Roubini described bitcoin as a Ponzi scheme.
[S06841] Legal scholar Eric Posner disagrees, however, as "a real Ponzi scheme takes fraud; bitcoin, by contrast, seems more like a collective delusion".
[S06842] A 2014 World Bank report also concluded that bitcoin was not a deliberate Ponzi scheme.
[S06843] Bitcoin markets operate 24 hours a day, seven days a week, contrasting with traditional financial markets that have fixed trading hours.
[S06844] Bitcoin prices show much higher volatility and respond strongly to both regulatory changes and market events.
[S06845] The volume of bitcoin trading can fluctuate considerably among various exchanges and geographic regions.
[S06846] The daily transaction volume of bitcoin across all exchanges typically reaches $50 billion as of 2025.
[S06847] *
*

*

* Code repository on Github 

 
Category:Cryptocurrencies
Category:Currencies introduced in 2009

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SOURCE: Wikipedia — Initial public offering
WORDS: 3,189
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
An '''initial public offering''' ('''IPO''') or '''stock launch''' is a public offering in which shares of a company are sold to institutional investors Note: the price the company receives from the institutional investors is the IPO price and usually also to retail investors.
[S06848] An IPO is typically underwritten by one or more investment banks, who also arrange for the shares to be listed on one or more stock exchanges.
[S06849] Through this process, colloquially known as '''''floating''''' or '''''going public''''', a privately held company is transformed into a public company.
[S06850] IPOs can be used to raise new equity capital for companies, to monetize the investments of private shareholders such as company founders or private equity investors, and to enable easy trading of existing holdings or future capital raising by becoming publicly traded.
[S06851] After the IPO, shares are traded freely in the open market at what is known as the free float.
[S06852] Stock exchanges stipulate a minimum free float both in absolute terms (the total value as determined by the share price multiplied by the number of shares sold to the public) and as a proportion of the total share capital (i.e., the number of shares sold to the public divided by the total shares outstanding).
[S06853] Although IPO offers many benefits, there are also significant costs involved, chiefly those associated with the process such as banking and legal fees, and the ongoing requirement to disclose important and sometimes sensitive information.
[S06854] Details of the proposed offering are disclosed to potential purchasers in the form of a lengthy document known as a prospectus.
[S06855] Most companies undertake an IPO with the assistance of an investment banking firm acting in the capacity of an underwriter.
[S06856] Underwriters provide several services, including help with correctly assessing the value of shares (share price) and establishing a public market for shares (initial sale).
[S06857] Alternative methods such as the Dutch auction have also been explored and applied for several IPOs.
[S06858] The earliest form of a company which issued ''public shares'' was the case of the ''publicani'' during the Roman Republic, although this claim is not shared by all modern scholars.
[S06859] Like modern joint-stock companies, the ''publicani'' were legal bodies independent of their members whose ownership was divided into shares, or ''parts''.
[S06860] There is evidence that these shares were sold to public investors and traded in a type of over-the-counter market in the Forum, near the Temple of Castor and Pollux.
[S06861] The shares fluctuated in value, encouraging the activity of speculators, or ''quaestors''.
[S06862] Mere evidence remains of the prices for which ''parts'' were sold, the nature of initial public offerings, or a description of stock market behavior.
[S06863] ''Publicani'' lost favor with the fall of the Republic and the rise of the Empire.
[S06864] In the United States, the first IPO was the public offering of Bank of North America around 1783.
[S06865] IPO procedures are governed by different laws in different countries.
[S06866] In the United States, IPOs are regulated by the United States Securities and Exchange Commission under the Securities Act of 1933.
[S06867] In the United Kingdom, the UK Listing Authority reviews and approves prospectuses and operates the listing regime.
[S06868] Planning is crucial to a successful IPO.
[S06869] Following highlights some general steps to planning Lipman, International and U.S. IPO Planning, (simplified, may have more steps):
# develop impressive management and professional team
# grow the company's business with an eye to the public marketplace
# obtain audited financial statements using IPO-accepted accounting principles
# clean up the company's act
# establish antitakeover defenses
# develop good corporate governance
# create insider bail-out opportunities and take advantage of IPO windows.
[S06870] IPOs generally involve one or more investment banks known as "underwriters".
[S06871] The company offering its shares, called the "issuer", enters into a contract with a lead underwriter to sell its shares to the public.
[S06872] The underwriter then approaches investors with offers to sell those shares.
[S06873] A large IPO is usually underwritten by a "syndicate" of investment banks, the largest of which take the position of "lead underwriter".
[S06874] Upon selling the shares, the underwriters retain a portion of the proceeds as their fee.
[S06875] This fee is called an underwriting spread.
[S06876] The spread is calculated as a discount from the price of the shares sold (called the gross spread).
[S06877] Components of an underwriting spread in an initial public offering (IPO) typically include the following (on a per-share basis): manager's fee, underwriting fee—earned by members of the syndicate, and the concession—earned by the broker-dealer selling the shares.
[S06878] The manager would be entitled to the entire underwriting spread.
[S06879] A member of the syndicate is entitled to the underwriting fee and the concession.
[S06880] A broker-dealer who is not a member of the syndicate but sells shares would receive only the concession, while the member of the syndicate who provided the shares to that broker-dealer would retain the underwriting fee.
[S06881] Usually, the managing/lead underwriter, also known as the bookrunner, typically the underwriter selling the largest proportions of the IPO, takes the highest portion of the gross spread, up to 8% in some cases.
[S06882] Multinational IPOs may have many syndicates to deal with differing legal requirements in both the issuer's domestic market and other regions.
[S06883] For example, an issuer based in the E.U.
[S06884] may be represented by the major selling syndicate in its domestic market, Europe, in addition to separate group corporations or selling them for US/Canada and Asia.
[S06885] Usually, the lead underwriter in the head selling group is also the lead bank in the other selling groups.
[S06886] Because of the wide array of legal requirements and because it is an expensive process, IPOs also typically involve one or more law firms with major practices in securities law, such as the Magic Circle firms of London and the white-shoe firms of New York City.
[S06887] Financial historians Richard Sylla and Robert E. Wright have shown that before 1860 most early U.S. corporations sold shares in themselves directly to the public without the aid of intermediaries like investment banks.
[S06888] Robert E. Wright, "Reforming the U.S. IPO Market: Lessons from History and Theory", ''Accounting, Business, and Financial History'' (November 2002), 419–437.
[S06889] The direct public offering (DPO), as they term it, Robert E. Wright and Richard Sylla, "Corporate Governance and Stockholder/Stakeholder Activism in the United States, 1790–1860: New Data and Perspectives".
[S06890] In Jonathan Koppell (ed.
[S06891] ), ''Origins of Shareholder Advocacy'' (New York: Palgrave Macmillan, 2011), 231–51.
[S06892] was not done by auction but rather at a share price set by the issuing corporation.
[S06893] In this sense, it is the same as the fixed price public offers that were the traditional IPO method in most non-US countries in the early 1990s.
[S06894] The DPO eliminated the agency problem associated with offerings intermediated by investment banks.
[S06895] The sale (allocation and pricing) of shares in an IPO may take several forms.
[S06896] Common methods include:
*Best efforts contract
*Firm commitment contract
*All-or-none contract
*Bought deal
Public offerings are sold to both institutional investors and retail clients of the underwriters.
[S06897] A licensed securities salesperson (Registered Representative in the US and Canada) selling shares of a public offering to their clients is paid a portion of the selling concession (the fee paid by the issuer to the underwriter) rather than by their client.
[S06898] In some situations, when the IPO is not a "hot" issue (undersubscribed), and where the salesperson is the client's advisor, it is possible that the financial incentives of the advisor and client may not be aligned.
[S06899] The issuer usually allows the underwriters an option to increase the size of the offering by up to 15% under a specific circumstance known as the greenshoe or overallotment option.
[S06900] This option is always exercised when the offering is considered a "hot" issue, by virtue of being oversubscribed.
[S06901] In the US, clients are given a preliminary prospectus, known as a red herring prospectus, during the initial quiet period.
[S06902] The red herring prospectus is so named because of a bold red warning statement printed on its front cover.
[S06903] The warning states that the offering information is incomplete, and may be changed.
[S06904] The actual wording can vary, although most roughly follow the format exhibited on the Facebook IPO red herring.
[S06905] During the quiet period, the shares cannot be offered for sale.
[S06906] Brokers can, however, take indications of interest from their clients.
[S06907] At the time of the stock launch, after the Registration Statement has become effective, indications of interest can be converted to buy orders, at the discretion of the buyer.
[S06908] Sales can only be made through a final prospectus cleared by the Securities and Exchange Commission.
[S06909] The final step in preparing and filing the final IPO prospectus is for the issuer to retain one of the major financial "printers", who print (and today, also electronically file with the SEC) the registration statement on Form S-1.
[S06910] Typically, preparation of the final prospectus is actually performed at the printer, wherein one of their multiple conference rooms the issuer, issuer's counsel (attorneys), underwriter's counsel (attorneys), the lead underwriter(s), and the issuer's accountants/auditors make final edits and proofreading, concluding with the filing of the final prospectus by the financial printer with the Securities and Exchange Commission.
[S06911] Before legal actions initiated by New York Attorney General Eliot Spitzer, which later became known as the Global Settlement enforcement agreement, some large investment firms had initiated favorable research coverage of companies in an effort to aid corporate finance departments and retail divisions engaged in the marketing of new issues.
[S06912] The central issue in that enforcement agreement had been judged in court previously.
[S06913] It involved the conflict of interest between the investment banking and analysis departments of ten of the largest investment firms in the United States.
[S06914] The investment firms involved in the settlement had all engaged in actions and practices that had allowed the inappropriate influence of their research analysts by their investment bankers seeking lucrative fees.
[S06915] A typical violation addressed by the settlement was the case of CSFB and Salomon Smith Barney, which were alleged to have engaged in the inappropriate spinning of "hot" IPOs and issued fraudulent research reports in violation of various sections within the Securities Exchange Act of 1934.
[S06916] A company planning an IPO typically appoints a lead manager, known as a bookrunner, to help it arrive at an appropriate price at which the shares should be offered.
[S06917] There are two primary ways in which the price of an IPO can be determined.
[S06918] Either the company, with the help of its lead managers, fixes a price ("fixed price method"), or the price can be determined through analysis of confidential investor demand data compiled by the bookrunner ("book building").
[S06919] Historically, many IPOs have been underpriced.
[S06920] The effect of underpricing an IPO is to generate additional interest in the stock and a rapid rise in share price when it first becomes publicly traded (known as an "IPO pop").
[S06921] Flipping, or quickly selling shares for a profit, can lead to significant gains for investors who were allocated shares of the IPO at the offering price.
[S06922] However, underpricing an IPO results in lost potential capital for the issuer.
[S06923] One extreme example is theglobe.com IPO which helped fuel the IPO "mania" of the late 1990s internet era.
[S06924] Underwritten by Bear Stearns on 13 November 1998, the IPO was priced at $9 per share.
[S06925] The share price quickly increased 1,000% on the opening day of trading, to a high of $97.
[S06926] Selling pressure from institutional flipping eventually drove the stock back down, and it closed the day at $63.
[S06927] Although the company did raise about $30&nbsp;million from the offering, it is estimated that with the level of demand for the offering and the volume of trading that took place they might have left upwards of $200&nbsp;million on the table.
[S06928] The danger of overpricing is also an important consideration.
[S06929] If a stock is offered to the public at a higher price than the market will pay, the underwriters may have trouble meeting their commitments to sell shares.
[S06930] Even if they sell all of the issued shares, the stock may fall in value on the first day of trading.
[S06931] If so, the stock may lose its marketability and hence even more of its value.
[S06932] This could result in losses for investors, many of whom being the most favored clients of the underwriters.
[S06933] Perhaps the best-known example of this is the Facebook IPO in 2012.
[S06934] Underwriters, therefore, take many factors into consideration when pricing an IPO, and attempt to reach an offering price that is low enough to stimulate interest in the stock but high enough to raise an adequate amount of capital for the company.
[S06935] When pricing an IPO, underwriters use a variety of key performance indicators and non-GAAP measures.
[S06936] The process of determining an optimal price usually involves the underwriters ("syndicate") arranging share purchase commitments from leading institutional investors.
[S06937] Some researchers (Friesen & Swift, 2009) believe that the underpricing of IPOs is less a deliberate act on the part of issuers and/or underwriters, and more the result of an over-reaction on the part of investors (Friesen & Swift, 2009).
[S06938] One potential method for determining to underprice is through the use of IPO underpricing algorithms.
[S06939] Other researchers have discovered that firms with higher revenues from licensing-based technology commercialization exhibit greater IPO underpricing, while a firm's stock of patents mitigates this effect.
[S06940] A Dutch auction allows shares of an initial public offering to be allocated based only on price aggressiveness, with all successful bidders paying the same price per share.
[S06941] One version of the Dutch auction is OpenIPO, which is based on an auction system designed by economist William Vickrey.
[S06942] This auction method ranks bids from highest to lowest, then accepts the highest bids that allow all shares to be sold, with all winning bidders paying the same price.
[S06943] It is similar to the model used to auction Treasury bills, notes, and bonds since the 1990s.
[S06944] Before this, Treasury bills were auctioned through a discriminatory or pay-what-you-bid auction, in which the various winning bidders each paid the price (or yield) they bid, and thus the various winning bidders did not all pay the same price.
[S06945] Both discriminatory and uniform price or "Dutch" auctions have been used for IPOs in many countries, although only uniform price auctions have been used so far in the US.
[S06946] Large IPO auctions include Japan Tobacco, Singapore Telecom, BAA Plc and Google (ordered by size of proceeds).
[S06947] A variation of the Dutch auction has been used to take a number of U.S. companies public including Morningstar, Interactive Brokers Group, Overstock.com, Ravenswood Winery, Clean Energy Fuels, and Boston Beer Company.
[S06948] In 2004, Google used the Dutch auction system for its initial public offering.
[S06949] Traditional U.S. investment banks have shown resistance to the idea of using an auction process to engage in public securities offerings.
[S06950] The auction method allows for equal access to the allocation of shares and eliminates the favorable treatment accorded important clients by the underwriters in conventional IPOs.
[S06951] In the face of this resistance, the Dutch auction is still a little used method in U.S. public offerings, although there have been hundreds of auction IPOs in other countries.
[S06952] In determining the success or failure of a Dutch auction, one must consider competing objectives.
[S06953] If the objective is to reduce risk, a traditional IPO may be more effective because the underwriter manages the process, rather than leaving the outcome in part to random chance in terms of who chooses to bid or what strategy each bidder chooses to follow.
[S06954] From the viewpoint of the investor, the Dutch auction allows everyone equal access.
[S06955] Moreover, some forms of the Dutch auction allow the underwriter to be more active in coordinating bids and even communicating general auction trends to some bidders during the bidding period.
[S06956] Some have also argued that a uniform price auction is more effective at price discovery, although the theory behind this is based on the assumption of independent private values (that the value of IPO shares to each bidder is entirely independent of their value to others, even though the shares will shortly be traded on the aftermarket).
[S06957] Theory that incorporates assumptions more appropriate to IPOs does not find that sealed bid auctions are an effective form of price discovery, although possibly some modified form of auction might give a better result.
[S06958] In addition to the extensive international evidence that auctions have not been popular for IPOs, there is no U.S. evidence to indicate that the Dutch auction fares any better than the traditional IPO in an unwelcoming market environment.
[S06959] A Dutch auction IPO by WhiteGlove Health, Inc., announced in May 2011 was postponed in September of that year, after several failed attempts to price.
[S06960] An article in ''the Wall Street Journal'' cited the reasons as "broader stock-market volatility and uncertainty about the global economy have made investors wary of investing in new stocks".
[S06961] Under American securities law, there are two-time windows commonly referred to as "quiet periods" during an IPO's history.
[S06962] The first and the one linked above is the period of time following the filing of the company's S-1 but before SEC staff declare the registration statement effective.
[S06963] During this time, issuers, company insiders, analysts, and other parties are legally restricted in their ability to discuss or promote the upcoming IPO (U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, 2005).
[S06964] The other "quiet period" refers to a period of 10 calendar days following an IPO's first day of public trading.
[S06965] During this time, insiders and any underwriters involved in the IPO are restricted from issuing any earnings forecasts or research reports for the company.
[S06966] When the quiet period is over, generally the underwriters will initiate research coverage on the firm.
[S06967] A three-day waiting period exists for any member that has acted as a manager or co-manager in a secondary offering.
[S06968] Not all IPOs are eligible for delivery settlement through the DTC system, which would then either require the physical delivery of the stock certificates to the clearing agent bank's custodian or a delivery versus payment (DVP) arrangement with the selling group firm.
[S06969] "Stag profit" is a situation in the stock market before and immediately after a company's initial public offering (or any new issue of shares).
[S06970] A "stag" is a party or individual who subscribes to the new issue expecting the price of the stock to rise immediately upon the start of trading.
[S06971] Thus, stag profit is the financial gain accumulated by the party or individual resulting from the value of the shares rising.
[S06972] This term is more popular in the United Kingdom than in the United States.
[S06973] In the US, such investors are usually called flippers, because they get shares in the offering and then immediately turn around "flipping" or selling them on the first day of trading.
[S06974] Prior to 2009, the United States was the leading issuer of IPOs in terms of total value.
[S06975] *Alternative public offering
*Direct public offering
*Public offering without listing
*Reverse takeover
*Smaller reporting company
*Special-purpose acquisition company
*Venture capital

[S06977] ''Exchanges Handbook''.
[S06978] Mondo Visione.
